(If you have a digital camera, though, with which to ensure you got the perfect engagement photos ... definitely bring it!)

I'm deducting a couple of stars for Knapp Castle's initial appearance as well as the sandy/rocky pathway; however, the fact that it provided a place for me to make a commitment to the kindest, handsomest, and overall most lovely person I've ever met makes me wish I had at least eighty stars to give.

Plus, there was a dog.

Beautiful place to watch the sunset. It was crowded with young college aged kids that weren't very considerate of those around.  Was tough to get around them and find spots for pictures. This place is basically ruins of a castle. There was a bulldozer there. Not sure what they're doing. Good view point.

This is an awesome place to take photographs or to hang out and enjoy a Santa Barbara sunset.  Hard to find, but once you get there the walk is short and not very difficult (I'm not in shape at all and I can walk it easily).  I've used this location for a number of photo shoots and have loved my results every time.  My most recent shoot was a few days ago and with just one hour before sunset, it felt like a very fast shoot.  I'd recommend closer to two hours to get the most out of the structures, walls, archs, and other cool features.
This review is for when I visited back in 2017. It's an interesting place to explore and enjoy nice views. We saw some people having a picnic, it looked like a good place to have one!

Very cool drive (it is part of a safety corridor so be careful) and amazing view. Actual walking is minimal as you park right outside.  Lots of people here, hard to get good pictures if you come at a busy time. Try in the mornings for the fresh air and solitude. The space itself is actually quite small. A must visit for Santa Barbara
Absolutely beautiful. The drive is pretty narrow around the canyons but it's definitely Worth it. The mountains are beautiful and if you're in the area, do this!

Didn't check the weather and it ended up raining the day we planned to visit this place, but we went anyway because I was so excited! 

It might be a little confusing if you don't do research ahead of time--scroll down and read Esther's review for the right coordinates. I entered them into my GPS and it led me right up to the place where the "trail" starts. From that point, you take the path beyond the red gate and it'll lead you directly to the ruins!

This place is technically not open to the public since it is private property, but it has become a tourist-ish attraction. You won't spend a great deal of time here, but it is a great place for photos. Since I visited on a cloudy/dark day, we weren't able to experience the sunset (the fog was really heavy), but it has a great view anyway!

If you're looking for a place to capture some solid IG "I'm one amongst nature photos" but you don't feel like hiking miles and miles at a National Park, or even waiting to get a lottery pick to visit a national park, then this is the place for you. 

For starters this isn't a hike, the "castle" is about 1/3 of a mile from where you park outside the gate (about a 5 minute walk). Most of the area itself looks like sh1t until you get to that wall where you have the background of the mountains. If you're there in the morning you can catch views with clouds inside the mountains, in the afternoon you can catch views with a bright sunny day, and later in the day you can catch some really good sunset views.

I wasn't expecting much for the location itself before I got here, & it really isn't much, but the views you can get in the background are worth it to come check out. And you should probably come sooner rather than later since there's some sort of construction going on here. I'm not sure if there building more stuff that will cover the view or taking the wall down but come here and get a geo tag of this location and be exclusive, unlike the 2 million plus basic squares that have the same stupid a$$ picture at Horse Shoe bend.

This is your chance to kind of be original..... this is your chance to be insta-famous..... go do it, and pick up your f#cking trash on the way out, some jerk left an empty 12 pack of model last week. Not cool bro.

Knapp Castle is private property. The trail head is the driveway entrance. There is an actual trail and it is on the Cachuma Lake side of the property. Partway down the driveway on the left the trail starts, the path goes downhill below the old house ruins and goes on for miles. 

The property is surrounded by Los Padres National Forest and to park a vehicle along any stretch of the road you need an Adventure Pass, which can be purchased in many stores in Santa Barbara including Big 5. http://goo.gl/17rrF3 The forest Service site lists where the passes may be purchased and can be purchased for one day or one year. There are 8 days in the year the fee is waived to the public so that the public can enjoy all the land has to offer.

I have a "DJI spark" the smallest drone ever. I was taking a quick video there when this very rude man that looked over the property told me to stop shooting. As he walked away he said "fuck all of you". I drove out of my way to enjoy this beautiful spot, that guy ruined the whole experience. He was rude to everyone there, I over heard other people complaining about him. If you're going there to get a nice shot or two, beware of a man with a white Chrysler van, he hates his life.

Me and my wife went to go see this just last weekend. The narrow road leading up to this spot is very winding and a little scary. Once you get to the area you have to park to the side of the road. A lot of the remains are still there but they are doing construction on the actual site now. There was a few construction guys there when we got there and he said that they were trying to rebuild the structure.  He said that the owner of the property had gotten the permits to build or rebuild the home.  It sounds like the remains may be demolished very soon. They had already built what looked like a brick wall foundation just around the ruins. I would urge you to go see it soon because it sounds like they may be demolishing what is left of the ruins there. The view is very beautiful once you get to the top.  The arches that are there are very beautiful. This is definitely a small short stop to a better destination. This isn't a spot where you would make a whole day trip out to. But you could definitely stop if you were on the way to Solvang or an offshoot from Santa Barbara.  We were only there for about half an hour.   We got there around 8 AM. There were people leaving who came to see it but once we got to the ruins we were the only ones there other than the three construction workers.  Again, if you were planning to see this I would urge you to go out soon because of the construction that is going on property.

Stop by this ruin on our camping trip. Worth the stop! It's up in the mountain. Park ur car by the side and walk few minutes to the ruin. Shouldnt be that hard to find since everyone has posted the direction and how to. 
Beautiful place with Santa Ynez river canyon overlook. 

Dont miss the swing and bathtub!

Don't get me wrong it's a beautiful scenic view but I wouldn't call this a hike, more of walking from parking lot to trail takes about 10 minutes. The drive up is extremely windy and narrow. It's good for a romantic sunrise and sunset and for photographer lovers. It's nice to see the ocean to Mountain View but it's not a whole day type of thing,  more of  pit stop if your in the area or on your way to a day trip solvang from Santa Barbara then it's on the same route

Hands down one of my most favorite activities for a date. Go up there on a nice sunny Day and you will have gorgeous views of the mountains and the valley. Don't wear crazy nice shoes but the hike is definitely very easy and I did it in flip flops. Beautiful sunset. Twisty roads. 

I highly recommend! Must see!

What a quick and lovely little hike.  We are avid hikers, so this felt more like a nature trail to us than a true hike, but based on the faces of other visitors, you should bring water if you are not accustomed to hiking.  We were fine in our regular athletic shoes and some people hiked in their flip flops, so anyone SHOULD be able to complete this hike.  It took us about 10 minutes of walking to arrive at the castle, and the payoff is tremendous.

We went on a weekday at sunset and saw about a dozen people, so I assume weekends here are packed.  As usual people were polite on the trail. There are no restrooms or water, this is technically private property and the owners are sharing it with us, so if you bring it in, bring it out.  

A final note: when you "hike in", you are going downhill most of the way, which means that on your way out, you're going uphill, if you struggle with elevation changes over slightly rocky terrain, you should sit this out. Medical attention may be hard to get immediately.

Ideal hike for a short/weekend trip to Santa Barbara. This hike was perfect for us as we were only spending one day in the area. Driving to the trail was easy, not hard to find at all, and ample space to park. The trail itself was short and simple, took about 15 minutes to get to Knapp's Castle. We spent about an hour there messing around and taking photos, most people we saw left about after 30 minutes. 

The ruins will not look like an abandoned castle because it is not! To be more precise, this place should really be called Knapp's house. There are several parts of the ruins you can climb on for that "daring Instagram" photo (#doitforthegram). For those of you that are afraid of heights, this is a great baby step forward in conquering that fear. Even my most timid of friends were able to get to the first portion of the abandoned archway.

Only complaint about this location is that there are SOOO many flies. Lot of the photos we took had us trying to swat them away. If you go, bring some bug spray/repellant.

Do not go here. First of all, the parking is not safe. There was broken glass all over the area off the side of the road where people park to come here. You can tell the broken glass was from Many different cars because of the way it was distributed. I thought it would be okay to park there for a few minutes, but as soon as I started walking on the trail, I heard two motorcycles pass, one of which drove slowly and looked over at us walking along the trail. This area is kind of deserted, and I thought it was strange to see them pass just shortly after we parked. So I came back to my car to watch it. 

I let my friend go alone to check out the place while I watched the car. Apparently, there is now a bulldozer and some other large object there that kind of ruins the effect. Since it didn't seem to be anything special, I didn't bother going myself.

This place is awesome!! It reminds me of Echo Mountain in L.A. but without the endless switchbacks. I believe the story is that someone rich built a place high up in the hills and it burned down. What we see now is the ruins of the place...and the 360 view of Lake Cachuma and mountains in the area.  

The views are absolutely breathtaking!! I wouldn't really classify this as a hike since it is just a very short walk to the ruins. Good for all ages. The drive itself is a bit more daunting - the roads are very windy and narrow. Since you are driving up a mountain, depending on the weather, the drive can be really hazy and cloudy, so please be careful and drive slow! The car is really doing most of the work, not your legs.

To get to Knapp's Castle, you take the 154. It is really easy to miss the turn into Camino Cielo since there isn't a major sign or stop, so set up your GPS to assist you. To make the drive a bit more worthy, stop by the Painted Caves also! 

Parking: need adventure pass (but I don't really know if anyone is checking)...parking is just on the side of the road. Hopefully there are other cars there so you don't miss it! 

Here's a helpful link for more info: http://www.hikespeak.com/trails/knapps-castle-hike-santa-barbara/

Easy little one mile walk if you take the long, winding driving route. (You can do a longer hike from down below if you choose). The short version is perfect for my pug who has a short tolerance for exercise, lol! Scenic view. Would love a romantic dinner date staged up here.
Amazing, beautiful, spectacular views!!! Would love to know more about the history of it all. Oh and my fiancé proposed to me here, so it holds a special place in our hearts forever.

Decided to drive to Knapp's Castle to visit the ruins and take a short hike while on our trip to Santa Barbara. It was a beautiful drive up but be warned that the roads are very winding. Our GPS took us past a gate with construction sign--which we confirmed with a hiker, was the road leading to the castle. We parked up the road and walked back to gate and path. After a short trek, a construction site with workers, came into view. We saw some remains of the original castle amidst the construction. Unsure if we were trespassing, we approached a worker and was told that Knapp's was undergoing construction by owner. Though the crew was working, he welcomed us to take a look around. We took in the amazing views and arches, took photos and then headed back. We saw recent concrete posts added, for possibly a private gate? Not sure with private construction whether or not Knapp's Castle will remain an open visitor site.

Kinda hard to find, but go with the GPS coordinates that someone else left here. You'll park along the road and then hike/walk about 10 minutes until you reach the place. Really nice views and totally worth the effort.
It's a fun little hike with views of the valley. It's a good place to go and take pictures but it's usually well populated and the last time I went there was some construction going on.

This is a very short hike. It was more like a road trip than a hike as the hike only took us 30 minutes and drive took us more than an hr. I live in LA in the San Fernando Valley. Once U park on the side of the road it's about half of mile to get to the castle. There's a sign that reads private property we still went through and there was a man that came out of nowhere who was not that friendly so we left as there is not much to see other that a broken down castle with a few walls.

Beautiful views toward Santa Ynez of the mountains .... same view I have from my home! We often ride our electric bikes by here but it is being renovated and I believe one day soon there will be someone living here!

Honestly, the "hike" is more of a walk to your neighbor's mailbox. Super short, you can see the ruins within 2 minutes of walking on the dirt and rocky path. Done in rainbow sandals. Ta-da. There isn't any information about the Castle on-site, so if you want to know the story behind it, I suggest looking it up. Pretty cool for a pit stop from Slo to Sb, but don't sit in the back of the car up the windy road if you get motion sickness easily.
CLOSED - A private residence is being built on this private property and access to the public has been discontinued.

Put in to google maps and could not find the damn place! Also lost cell reception at the top (Verizon!!) so could not double check where we were meant to be. Did not see any signs for the castle either.... plus google maps sent us up a dangerous thin windy road via the mountain side with blind corners. Freakin scary and not fun for anyone!! 

Very disappointing. 

Best of luck if you can find it!!

As of 7/9/2020

They are not allowing visitors at all due to the construction going on. We hiked up and weren't able to get pass the crew unfortunately! Keep that in mind when you're heading up. The views to the peak were incredible though!

This isn't a "real" attraction. Getting to the castle is sketchy and it's rather obvious that you're trespassing on private property that is under construction. When my wife and I visited last year, there was a construction crew so we didn't approach as closely as I've seen in some of the pictures here. After reading other reviews, I'm glad we didn't because it sounds like visitors aren't welcome. The structures we could see looked really neat, but I wouldn't recommend a visit.
That being said, our hike to get closer was gorgeous. we were just walking on a dirt road but the valley it overlooks is stunning as is the area we parked, just outside of the gate. It was amazing to watch the clouds move among the mountains.

The view during sunset (and in general) is amazing. Driving there is really not that bad, and I've always been able to find a parking spot even when it was crowded. Be mindful that this is technically private property, and people live nearby. My friend was absentmindedly throwing rocks off the trail once and we heard a voice down below telling us to knock it off. So just be respectful!! 

Overall, this is the perfect place to take people who are new to the area, or anyone who hasn't been here. It's about a 5 minute walk to the castle, which isn't really a castle, but it still looks cool. If you're looking for something romantic, you could definitely set up a picnic here and watch the sunset, but just do it on a weekday when it's not crowded.
Great little spot to take a short hike. The area is easy to find and picture perfect for the social media  picture people (guilty)!  It's worth the trip, about 25 min outside of Santa Barbara.

If you can get here before sunrise or sunset, I promise you will not be disappointed. The drive up the mountain is full of twists and turns, but you're rewarded with such a beautiful and serene view. Like the other reviews said, you park on the side of the road and hike down a super easy, short hike to Knapps Castle. 

When we arrived around 6-7am, there was already someone there waiting for the sun to rise. Another person came shortly after us with his dog and began meditating. It is definitely the place for a peaceful morning and to enjoy the views. 

I would recommend coming here for the views, but not if you're looking for a more intense hike.

Knapp's Castle was one of the few places I went while visiting Santa Barbara for the very first time. After that, I was easily sold on moving here. Fast-forward four years to present day, I now call Santa Barbara home.

The "Castle" itself is now stone ruins from what use to be a large home built in 1916 owned by George Owen Knapp, the founder of Union Carbide Chemical Corporation. Knapp's Castle was originally a three-story hunting "lodge" (more so mansion of its times) that contained 5 bedrooms (including guest houses, servants and caretakers), an organ room, an observatory, a pool and a cider mill near the apple orchard down the mountain. The chemical magnate "struck gold" when he sold the property in 1940 to a woman named Frances Holden, later to have it burn down only five weeks later in a forest fire! Since then, the property has been bought and sold on and off; one reason being the lack of a water source. Last locals had heard, Cal Smith is the current owner and historic landmark preservationist who plans to rebuild with RPM Architects with the goals of opening the property up as a teahouse. Read the full story here: https://keyt.com/news/2019/07/19/historic-landmark-preservationist-rebuilding-knapps-castle/

Regardless of its past, the structure's ruins sit on the mountain top of the Santa Ynez Mountain Range, part of Los Padres National Forest, with scenic 360-degree views of Lake Cachuma and the Santa Ynez Valley. From the main road where you park, it is a short walk on a dirt driveway that had to be built by Knapp himself to access the lodge. This is not technically a hike but more of a stroll, yet with quite the pay off. Between the ruins of a stone fireplace, more recently constructed amphitheatre steps, and a swing hanging from a eucalyptus tree, Knapp's Castle has become man's playground.

Knapp's Castle is the ideal place for a:
- Romantic date
- Family picnic
- Photoshoot (an engagement, wedding, graduation, maternity, etc)
- Instagram shots
- Music video (already done so you won't be the first)
- Sunset view
- Practically anything...
Just please clean up after yourself and pets to maintain what is so special for others to experience! Disregard the "Private Property" warning sign two-thirds down the path.

Directions (approximately 30 minutes from Santa Barbara): Drive using GPS coordinates (34.5183483, -119.7933454) as the final destination. This will take you up the 154/San Marcos Pass and have you turn right on East Camino Cielo Drive. You'll continue driving until the road becomes narrow and windy yet nothing else around but views. You'll end up at a metal gate where you park your car on the side of the road and walk down a dirt road to the "Castle." Lock your doors and hide any valuables, often cars are broken into here.

On your way to or from, be sure to stop by the less visited Chumash Painted Caves State Historic Park (https://bit.ly/2URMkLr).

So awesome that one man's misfortune can turn into some thing so cool for so many people.
Knapp's Castle was one man's little dream lodge located up in the hills above Santa Barbara. He built his dream only to have it burnt down a month later by a forest fire. 
What's left is an awesome place to take photos at. Beautiful panaramic vistas of untouched mountains and Lake Cachuma. The ruins of the lodge are so much fun to use as a photo prop. Nice stone archways, stairs, walls, fireplaces and that beautiful backdrop.... just Heaven!
Knapp's is located about 14 miles up the 154 San Marcos Pass from the 101 Highway. It's kind of a steep up hill climb. The road to the Castle is about ten miles in. You make a right on East El Camino Cielo. It's not as hard to miss as I thought it would be. El Camino Cielo is very narrow and winding, but the view of Santa Barbara and the Pacific Ocean is incredible. I'm not sure exactly how to describe how to find the exact location because my GPS couldn't find it, but one of the people I was with, his iPhone GPS was able to locate it AND the trail. I just know you drive until there is somewhat of a fork in the road. The only way to go is left because the other way is a dirt road that's gated off. Look for a small gate on the left hand side of the road that's not too much further. Knapp's is a short hike from there. There is a shoulder to park on. 
I didn't expect to see other people there, but there was a guy with a bunch of kids with him when we got to the site. Later on a couple of bicyclist showed up, too. There's a lot of trails around that I have no idea where they could go to. I kept hearing people, but couldn't see them. 
If you google Knapp's Castle you'll will find better information that I am giving. Apparently, people go up there at night, which I would be scared to do because there's no artificial light and that road was a little sketch to drive during the day. I also read somewhere that a bunch of hippies or something go up there when there is a full moon and beat drums. 
You will need to buy an Adventure Pass that you can get from Big 5 that costs $5. I don't know who checks if you have one or not, but it's better to be safe than sorry. It's upto a $100 fine if you don't have one. 
I hope to someday come back here. It's a wonderful little place to hangout.

Knapp's is a definite must see for SB County locals and those traveling through that have the extra time. Wouldn't recommend it as a #1 must see spot, but very cool if the time is available. 

It is a long drive up the 135 into Santa Ynez area, which is very beautiful and consists of gorgeous views of Santa Barbara & Goleta (if you're coming from the South). Make sure you have plenty gas, though, because the hills are genuinely straight up hill on the way up. Once up top, though, it is worth the extra funds at the pump. 

From downtown it takes about 20-30 minutes to get to the parking area and another 7-10 minutes to walk to the castle ruins. I wouldn't call it a hike, but also would not recommend walking in heels or anything of the sorts. It's dirty, dusty, and slightly hilly so bring your comfy shoes and change into the heels once you're up there if you are doing a photo shoot. 

On that note... This spot is GREAT for photo shoots! Prom, engagement, graduation, Saturday is for the boys - Anything you'd want to be epically photographed you should do here. I came with a photographer friend and did a few graduation pictures on a really ugly, cloudy, breezy day and they came out incredible anyways because of how legit the location and scenery is (and the photographer was awesome, check her out at Ranucci Photography).

Overall, must see for locals and can/should visit if there's time for tourists. Look up the history (or read reviews below) and maybe it'll mean even more to you then encouraging you to be a part of SB County culture in visiting this beautiful landmark.

Absolutely beautiful place to visit. A little difficult to get to but nevertheless the map directions will lead you to the entrance and it's roughly a 10 minute walk to get to the ruins. There is no description of the place or placard describing the history of the house. It has a swing nearby as well with a tremendous view of the Santa Ynez riverbed.

This is definitely  a cute little hike. I couldn't call it a hike though,  it's about a mile long walk.  So if you're looking for a good workout,  this isn't it.  But it's a very pleasant walk,  and even you get the castle remains its very picturesque! Lots of good photo opportunities.  And really beautiful views. 

I had never been here before but I saw families, they were smart,  they brought little picnics. Definitely  easy enough for little children to accomplish, and it seems fairly safe enough.  No major cliffs for them to fall off. 

The was multiple areas  to explore.  My favorite was the rope swing,  and climbing to the edge of one of the last remaining parts of the structure.  

I'd  come here for a nice view or maybe like the others,  a nice picnic.

Nice spot to take a scenic drive up the mountain and take pictures at. The road to get up to the top is very windy so if you easily get car sick it may be a difficult drive for you. I also would only go when it's very dry because the road is pretty narrow and there aren't guardrails.

The GPS led us right to the closed gate. We parked on the opposite side of the road and walked across. There's a stone path that leads down about a 1/4 mile or so to the ruins. It looks like the castle ruins are under construction of some sort as there is equipment and lots of boards at the site. 

We spent about 25 minutes there enjoying the views and taking a few pictures. On a Sunday afternoon there were about 6-8 other people there. We just had to wait a few minutes for a clear space to get some pictures.

Glad we took time out of our day for some beautiful views!!

Beautiful place. Be aware that this place has been purchased and the owner is building a home there and it is private property.  The owner was kind enough to let us tour around and enjoy the old ruins. Thank you so much for this privilege. From Chatsworth, ca.

GREAT view, SHORT hike, but HARD TO FIND!

Input these direct coordinates on your GPS: (34.51855, -119.793234)

As you are driving up the long hill that seems to be deserted (at one point, the road looks like it turned into a one-lane pathway!). Once you get there, don't fret. Just keep driving up the hill a few miles until you spot a rustic brown/red gate on your left. That will be the entrance to the 0.8 mile walk-hike to the beautiful ruins! The reason why I gave it 4 stars instead of 5 is the difficulty in finding this place.

So we took a drive up there to take a look at this place I did not know it's a private establishment. And sadly there is this house being built right in front of the Knapp Castle.  We did get to see it for only a few seconds until someone came out to kick us out because it's a private residence now theyre building this ugly home there sadly.

Definitely recommend coming here! Came here after visiting the Lizard's mouth as they weren't too far apart (~20 minute drive). The drive itself is a little windy but it's kind of expected as you're going up the mountains for views haha. I clicked on the address that was on yelp and it took me here via apple maps just fine. 

PARKING: You know you've reached the location when you see a gate. When I came, there was also a sign that said "no parking" on the same side of the gate. So, everyone just parked on the opposite side which was easy and plentiful. 

HIKE: This isn't really a hike. From where we parked, it took us a solid 8 minutes to get to the castle haha and we were definitely taking our sweet time. Very short "hike" (more like a stroll haha) and you can see the "castle" after you walk the first bend in the trail. Definitely doable for kid's and is overall pretty safe. 

KNAPP'S CASTLE: The castle itself isn't much, so I would keep expectations low. There actually was a construction truck and supplies on site when we visited. I am assuming they are trying to rebuild the castle or build over it. With that being said, I'd come here sooner than later as we may not know how much longer this place will exist. The view is beautiful here and we were able to climb on the arches. Be safe!! 

DRONE: I flew my drone here as there were no signs against it and it was perfect because not too many people were there (only 3 people). So much fun!

SWING: There are 2 swings to the left of the castle (when you're approaching it). You can get a picture with a view while on the swing. One swing is a traditional sit swing, but there's no board to sit on. I got a bruise on both sides of my hip because I swung on it too much LOL, so be careful haha. The other swing is foot one, where you can step your foot into it and stand while holding the rope. This was harder to get momentum to swing further out for a picture with the view, but hopefully we can figure it out.

A gorgeous, easy, simple trail from the roadside to the cliff side.

The hardest thing about Knapp's was finding the entrance but when we did, it was literally a walk in the park to the destination.

Will definitely be back again!

The steep drive from and to may be more stunning than the actual Knapp's Castle. 

The day of my hike, it was overcast with very gray clouds. From the Castle, the view was dark and cold, still pretty but not as pretty as the view on the opposite side. 

The hike is very short with little to no incline. It is a nice little hike to get the heart pumping a little. There are dozens of rocks sticking out of the ground so be careful and watch your step. The hike may not get the blood flowing but it can cause a trip!

Parking is free but depending on the amount of visitors, it can be tricky. There is not designated parking lot and most of the parking spots are a few inches away from the edge of the cliff, so once again be careful!

I did not want a challenging hike and Knapp's Castle was perfect! It wasn't too far from State Street either which was a great place to grab a drink and grub!

went to check out the ruins today and found a construction site! looks like they're building a house or something. the ruins aren't there anymore and if they are, they aren't recognizable. wouldn't recommend hiking out here

***Just a bit of history before you get there!***
"George Owen Knapp, founder of Union Carbide, built Knapp's Castle shortly after purchasing the 160-acre parcel in 1916. In 1940, Frances Holden bought the property and invited her friend, world-famous opera singer Lotte Lehmann, to move in. The mansion was destroyed by a forest fire only five weeks later, and now only the massive sandstone foundations, fireplace pillars and walls of the original seven structures remain intact" (wikipedia). 


The road itself definitely caused some motion sickness, but pretty views all around! You will need your GPS and might lose service so screenshot the directions! 
Once you get there you will most likely see some cars parked on the side of the road so that should give away that you indeed did get to the right place. There will be a pretty wide and rocky pathway to the castle, but its not difficult at all! Just take some water, especially if its hot out! the pathway will split into two different directions one going a bit uphill and the other going downhill, you must go uphill to get to the castle. 

I felt like I was walking into an old time movie it was unreal! It was smaller than I expected it to be, but it didn't take away from the surrealness of it all. Its a worthwhile place to go, esp. if you're a sucker for views!!!

Am I there yet?
Getting there takes forever, I think we drove for a good 10-15 minutes before reaching the destination. Super narrow roads and a lot of twist and turns. So drive cautiously. 

Parking: 
To the side of the road, basically make your own parking spot. 

Experience: 
Not much of a hike in my opinion... more like a short stroll to this magnificent view! 

Came here on a Sunday afternoon during Labor Day Weekend and there were already two groups at the site. Be ready to wait to get onto the ledges of the arches and etc ! 

Everyone there was super nice and accommodating though when taking pictures! 

Tips: 
Perfect for a date, on my way down I saw a couple bring up a picnic basket and a bottle of wine with them. Super romantic place to be. 

Final Verdict:  
Thumbs up.

Resting atop a rocky point you'll find the ruined stone structure known as Knapp's Castle.  Despite the fact that the very short hike from Camino Cielo road won't give you much of a workout, its still totally worth the half hour drive here from Santa Barbara.  That's because the view from the Castle is both stunning and incredibly beautiful.

Tip: The views here are the most impressive at either sunrise or sunset.  I came for my 1st visit right before sunrise, and the experience was almost religious; everything was lovely and quite serene.

All that remains of the Castle are the fireplaces, rock arches, foundation, chimneys, and stairways; the Paradise Canyon Fire consumed the rest of the original structure in 1940.  Here's what Wikipedia has to say about this magical venue:

"George Owen Knapp, founder of Union Carbide, built Knapp's Castle shortly after purchasing the 160-acre parcel in 1916. In 1940, Frances Holden bought the property and invited her friend, world-famous opera singer Lotte Lehmann, to move in. The mansion was destroyed by a forest fire only five weeks later, and now only the massive sandstone foundations, fireplace pillars and walls of the original seven structures remain intact. The parcel is still privately owned but open to the public."

Bearing in mind that Knapp's Castle isn't a hike so much as a lovely and historic vista point, you might also enjoy the drive here; it takes you along roads with a plethora of twists and turns, and ultimately rewards you with some truly stunning and unique views.

I'm doing 5 stars for the view and the structure which are amazing. Great location for photographs and relaxing. The walk/hike to the actual structure is less than a mile and very easy to do. Unfortunately, several vehicles have been broken into on the side of the road where you park. I got lucky and nothing happened to my vehicle.
During my visit most visitors were quiet and exploring like myself until ......Here comes the raider nation to be loud and obnoxious thinking this place is a carnival.....Ugh. 
Worth the drive and the short walk.

Cal Smith re-building Knapp's Castle. Hikers and visitors welcome and pictures encouraged! Remember this is a construction site , so please use good judgement and do not walk under workers or become a nuisance so that others can enjoy this site. Having just visited The Big Yellow House, where visitors are not welcome, Franceschi Park which awaits demolition of main house and Timbers Restaurant which is closed and forgotten - we the public have hit a home run. A preservationist who is inclusive and is bringing the past to life. 
P.S.Swing still open as of  10/11/2019

Great find in Santa Barbara! Decided to come here to take some pictures for my birthday. Arrived just a bit before sunset and the views were spectacular. This is a great location for a photo shoot. 

Your GPS will direct you to turn left when arriving at the destination but you'll noticed a closed off gate. Park on the right side and make your way through the gate. It'll be a short 10-15 minute hike from that point.

I did a photoshoot today for free btw. Some shirtless man comes to me and my friends and told us we cannot shoot there without asking permission. He points at a sign that Is behind a bush that says that... okay.. so I have to drive all the way up here to get a phone number and ask permission to take pictures..... lol. Smh. I look around me and See a couple of photographers taking pictures.... but doesn't approach them. He claimed to be a caretaker... he might of been, but talk to other people that have a production going on in front of you instead of snapping at me and my friends because you see her wearing a pretty dress.   I apologized if he thought we were taking advantage of the place and the jerk rolled his eyes at me. At that point I'm livid, but happy that we were finished with the shoot lol. Knapp's castle on yelp does not even have a contact number or a website to visit. I suggest whoever the owners are or "caretaker" to invest setting that up before you go around harassing people just because "you thought or assumed" that I was shooting a wedding. They were engagements and they were friends of mine. The place is beautiful but these people are ridiculous. So if you are a photographer that wants to do a shoot here, all you have to do is drive up a scary mountain, park on a narrow street that your car can fall off from, hike 15 minutes all the way to the castle, read a sign on your right that is behind a bush, then leave and call that number and make an appt where you probably have to pay money that is not worth it.  That man needs to put a shirt on btw. Gross.

This place is spectacular from the ruins to the amazing view. We want twice. The first time there was a storm and it was raining pretty good so they shut the location down and was kicking everyone out due to safety hazards. The second time it was absolutely perfect! It's is a bit of a hike on a dirt trail to get their but once you arrive it is breathtaking. There is a rope swing which my daughter loved. She swung for awhile until another couple came and she got off to allow them a try.

In the mountains above Santa Barbara, a plot of privately owned land surrounded by the Los Padres National Forest. On that land stand the remains of a palatial estate that burned down seventy years ago, the so-called Knapp's Castle. To the ruins and back is a 0.8-mile round trip hike with 50 feet of elevation change. The majestic views from this unique spot far exceed the effort.

From East Camino Cielo at an elevation of 2,900 feet, take an unmarked dirt road down the north side of the mountain. After a quarter mile, as Knapp's Castle comes into view, turn right, passing through a gate to reach the ruins.

On a clear day you can stride down the imperial landing overlooking the Santa Ynez River Canyon, Lake Cachuma, and the opposing peaks of the San Rafael Mountains. Explore the enduring remains of Knapp's Castle, which include stone steps, archways, and fireplaces. Several pieces still stand from the impressive home built in 1916 for George Knapp, a former chairman for Union Carbide.

You can look northwest through a trio of stone arches toward Lake Cachuma, a reservoir that did not exist when Knapp's Castle was built or burned. While the view has certainly changed, this is still a breathtaking place to build a home or take a hike. When you have finished checking out the ruins, return the way you came.

No permit is required. To get to the trailhead: From Highway 101 in Santa Barbara, take Route 154 north for ten miles. At the top of the mountain, turn right on East Camino Cielo and drive three miles east to a small turnout on the right opposite the gated dirt road on the left (the start of the trail). The trailhead is located on a C-shaped bend in East Camino Cielo, one mile past Painted Cave Road, the turn off for Chumash Painted Cave State Historic Park.

This short hike was well worth the fast and furious drive up the mountainside. It's a pretty short distance so you won't work out much of a sweat. I survived wearing my short-heel boots so that tells you how hard the hike is (though I would've preferred to walk in my normal running shoes but I forgot them at home). 

The view of Santa Barbara is pretty magical, especially during the early morning or late afternoon. It's a great spot for IG photos, if you haven't already noticed from Yelp pictures. 

Direction: if you put the address that Yelp provides on Waze or Google Maps, it'll tell you to drive through the closed fence, where the trail starts. Obviously, don't drive through it but just park outside on the street.

Disappointed to find out a new mansion is currently in construction over the Knapp Castle site.
However, the fire road up to the site has some nice views of the valley of Cachuma Lake area.

Beautiful drive on the way to and from the trail head!  We found the coordinates on the hikespeak website when we googled hiking near santa barabara.  There is a brown metal gate on the Left that blocks off a road.  This is the road you take but you cannot drive down.  You have to park on the R side of the main road and walk down this gated road to reach the castle.  BUT there is NO sign at all.  There happened to be one other couple parking at the same time as us who knew where to go otherwise we would never have found it!  Cool ruins and great views of the mountains!

Rating this is super hard because it really depends on what you are looking for.  This is a very short hike off the main road so if you are looking for a hike this isn't it.  The hike is probably between a quarter and a half mile long.  The place is in ruins but it's not an ancient building it had the remnants of an electrical system as well as steal beams bracing the walls.  That all being said the views are fantastic and it is cool wandering around what is left of the structure.

Poor man's Hearst Castle - 
It would be a stretch to say this is anything other than just a pile of rubble and rocks. Seems to also be a dumping ground too, i.e. the boat dumped off the side of the mountain. But the VIEW! The drive and the views make it worth your time. Nice tiny hike too.. Just put in Knapp's castle in google maps and looks for the gate and the parked cars.. no signage! 

Tip - Be sure to check out the Chumash Painted Cave while visiting here. It's not too far away.

Definitely go here! The drive alone is beautiful but also a bit scary. You can get directions via Google Maps and it's roughly a 30 min drive from the beach in Santa Barbara. We went for sunset, but I imagine that sunrise would also be very beautiful. Keep in mind that you park about a quarter of a mile from the actual spot and will need to walk that last bit. I recommend heading to the store, grabbing some picnic-type food, and enjoying a romantic, peaceful dinner while watching the sunset over the mountains.

Also, this place is super Instagram-worthy!

Knapp's Castle has all the right ingredients for an ideal hike; interesting historic background, explorable ruins, and breathtaking scenic views. My favorite part was taking in the views of the Santa Ynez River Valley through the frame of the castle's arched ruins. The pictures do not do it justice. I could just imagine what it's like at sunset. 

I suppose the biggest downside is it's distance. Being less than a mile roundtrip, you really don't break a sweat. I suppose the upside is that it is short enough that you can bring along the kids. There is even a rope swing for them to play on.

The nearest gas station and bathrooms are over 20 miles away so come prepared. Parking isn't the greatest but it's free. The day I visited it was pretty empty. It appears to be dog friendly. Definitely worth a visit.

We were in Santa Barbara for a couple of days and this was on my To-Do list. 

I read through the Yelp reviews and input the coordinates into Google Maps. Drive with caution! The road was windy, twisty and narrow but once you get closer to the property, it widens up.

There's some dirt you can park on beside the road across the property. It's all dirt and rocks/pebbles so wear comfy shoes that you don't mind getting dirty. Also, there isn't much shade so wear sunscreen and a hat.

Some mentioned that there were flies but we saw none. The walk is about ten minutes away from the road. There was one other car while we were there and we got there around 11.

The ruins are quite nice but the view got me. The only thing that killed the vibe was the sound of a bulldozer. Idk what they're doing there, but there were big metal containers and someone's truck while we were there and it looked like they were digging trenches around the property. There was a sign posted about trespassing and stuff and how they have cameras but how? 

All in all, we only stayed about 20 minutes.

This is pretty much a wilderness/park area. There's not much to see when there aren't any monarch butterflies. The monarchs arrive in the fall and depart in the spring. I saw a monarch the day before at Lotusland, so I had hopes of seeing them here, but there weren't any.

Try walking through the tree area towards the ocean. There a mesa to cross, but it's not that wide before getting to the coastal bluffs and then down to the beach - although this is technically not part of the Butterfly Preserve.

In my best Huell Howser voice, "Coronado Butterfly Preserve is definitely one of California's Gold".

Although there were not many butterflies on our jaunt through the shoreline, the short walk through the preserve was peaceful, relaxing, and therapeutic. I've had family live in SB for over 25 years and this is one of my favorite places to stop whenever I come up for a visit. Great for couples and families of all ages.

The main trail (and numerous off shoot trails) are all very well maintained and are wide enough for 2 people to walk side by side. Dogs are also permitted in this area as long as they are leashed. After a short walk you will be at the shoreline taking in the beautiful Santa Barbara coastline. If you're into trail running then this is the place for you. The shoreline trail is a perfect place to get a good run in and take in the sights!

There is a main parking lot that is relatively easy to find. If you park in the residential area then you might be able to find a spot with an even shorter walk to the shoreline.

So if you are looking for a break from the hustle and bustle of downtown Santa Barbara, State Street, and the packed beaches off Cabrillo, then Coronado Butterfly Preserve is a must visit.

In March there aren't many butterflies, but this is still a pretty nice walk. 

When you're driving down Hollister just turn when you see the sign for the preserve. There is a nice big parking lot and doggy bags/a trash can if you bring your pet. The trail veers off in several places so you have a few choices. If you take the most direct route to the beach it follows a dirt trail through trees, then a prettier trail through a wooded area and then you make a right up an asphalt hill that leads to the the trails on the cliffs above the beach. I'd say this is about a mile one way. 

I will definitely be back when the butterflies are around or hopefully after a rain so it's not so dry. 

Note: I don't think this place is off leash, but many people had their dogs off leash so I think it's okay.

My boyfriend surprised me with a trip to Santa Barbara specifically to see the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. He has heard me go on and on about how as a child, growing up in Stockton I'd see so many butterflies. It makes me sad that I don't see them as often in my city due to the drastic weather changes over the years. I literally yelp for joy if I do come across butterflies because it's just something I've held as a childhood memory that made me really happy. Regardless of me catching butterflies and not knowing that the powder in my hands could kill the butterfly... ANYWAYS. THAT'S NOT THE POINT.

We got to the parking lot, which is directly in front of Elwood Elementary School (there is no specific address provided on the website, just the coordinates). It has a large map at the west end of the lot to show you how easy it is to get to the preserve. Also, for those who feel like they may have lost their way, there are actual sign posts with the preserve's logo with butterflies on there to help you keep track of the trail. These trails are dusty as you're walking down to the preserve so plan accordingly. Dogs are allowed on the trails! Pay attention to the signs as you walk through the preserve because, while I did see tons of dogs on the trails, make sure that they are acceptable! If you let them off leash and are planning on going towards the beach after the preserve, keep a close eye on them because the cliffs are extremely blunt and they can easily mistake the edge of a cliff for a continued path... You have been warned!

At this time of year (late October), there were not tons of butterflies BUT there are a few of them! Every minute or so, we would spot two or three frolicking pretty high up in the trees. The Fall season is just starting, so as we continue into the winter I believe the populations of butterflies will multiply exponentially. As an extra precaution, if this destination is the only reason why you're coming here to Santa Barbara, do your research upon coming so you aren't disappointed in the time and gas you've spent to see patches of butterflies on trees because I didn't see them. With the drought, there are less butterflies because they depend heavily on the humidity within the air.

While SB is off of the coast, it was not extremely cold out here. I was able to remove my sweater and wear shorts and still find myself sweating!


In regards to the nameless beach that we stumbled upon, I have definitely visited better ones. The trails leading out of the preserve go directly to the beach. (To keep track of your whereabouts, screenshot your location on Google Maps since it shows the trails. This was how I was easily able to get back to the parking lot and roam far into the beaches without getting lost)

There were tons of dildo looking creatures washing ashore as I was beach combing for the nonexistent sand dollars! They have clear exterior with a pink interior, which spits out water if you comb it with a stick or something. I did it out of curiosity. They aren't sea cucumbers, but I don't know the names of them. No matter how far up or down the beach you go, these monsters lurk not too far from your feet! THEY WERE SURFING IN THE WAVES AS WELL so yeah. Just saying.

On top of the disgusting dildo-like creatures, there was a bunch of tar on the sand... As my boyfriend and I were leaving and about to put on our shoes, our feet were caked with tar! We couldn't remove it until we got home, which was an hour and a half away from SB! Oh, the insanity... We felt so uncomfortable. We hear people rave about Santa Barbara and the beauty of the beaches... only to be disappointed with the locals' unnecessary attitudes* and the thickly layered tar on our feet. With my experiences here, I would not recommend people coming to Santa Barbara specifically for the beaches or the preserve (unless there is an abundance of butterflies).

*As my boyfriend and I were walking back from the beach to the parking lot, we encountered extremely rude people. A man, who appeared to be a local, was riding his bike that had a cow bell to let people know that he was coming their way. Obviously, I heard it. My boyfriend and I decided to choose a path so that we wouldn't get in his way because we were both going in the same direction. Arrogantly, he decided to cut a corner and choose our path and said "Oops," as he almost crashed into me. There were three different paths going in the same direction! What the f?

Not even a minute later, these two guys (one Asian and the other some sort of Caucasian) were on their bikes and did the same THING! We specifically got out of their way and they both split up, one hooked left and the other right, rode two inches away from us. So unnecessary and I hope they burst into flames....

This is a nice place for stroll. 

Attempted to find this place via Google Maps and was just given a general idea of where to go. We wound up just parking in the adjacent residential area and walking towards the tall gathering of trees. If it weren't for the handful of monarch butterflies hovering around the entrance of the trail, we would have kept on walking and wondering if we were in the right place. 

It was cloudy when the BF and I went, even with the cloud cover it was about 81F at 11am and was a lot warmer than what we anticipated. The trail forks in several directions when you head in the direction of the ocean. BF and I walked through the trail leading into the tree grove(the signs will direct you), hoping to find butterflies galore inside, but we only came across a handful. Nonetheless, it was still a enjoyable walk. 

We may come back when we're at Bacara again. Hopefully, ready to conquer the trail leading towards the bluffs. Crossing my fingers to see the groves of Monarchs that visit the place.
Fun & peaceful hike. Sad that I wasn't able to see any butterflies, I'm sure we were late in the season. But Gorgeous views. Tons of perfect spots for pictures!

What an AMAZING way to spend a Saturday morning. 

We'd been meaning to visit earlier in the winter, but I'm glad we waited. The butterflies were up and about, flying around the grove. Being surrounded by thousands of free flying Monarch Butterflies is definitely a unique experience that I recommend to everyone reading this review. 

TIPS:

- The Ellwood grove and Coronado Reserve are the same thing. I was confused at first and thought they were different places. 
- If you continue past the grove, you will find a beautiful bluff overlooking the ocean. 
- It's not a hike, but I'd wear comfy shoes because the ground is muddy. 
- I read that by March, the butterflies have left Goleta and continued on their migration towards Mexico.

Directions:

From Highway 101, exit Storke Rd./Glen Annie and head south (away from mountains). Turn right on Hollister Avenue, drive about 1.2 miles and take a left on Coronado Drive. Entrance to the Preserve is at the end of Coronado Drive.
I moved away but always make sure to visit every year in February.  More butterflies in one place than you will ever see again!  Great place to bring kids and let them learn about nature.
There are several trails that lead to the beach and we got lost! Some trails are not an easy hike for families.

This place is soooo peaceful, beautiful, amazing, and magical!   We went around 10am and the butterflies were everywhere!   It was incredible. I felt like I was in a dream with all of the butterflies fluttering around. My boyfriend and I followed the path up towards the bluff overlooking the ocean which lead to an amazing view. We even saw these huge condors flying over head!  There were people running with their dogs and none of them were on leashes which I loved. 

It was an awesome Saturday mornign. 

Tips: it's not a hard core hike. Comfy shoes help, but you could easily go in jeans and sandals and be perfectly fine.

Beautiful "forest"!  We go most every year, and I just enjoy the peace and tranquility this place has to offer. Unfortunately, this year we didn't get to experience the butterfly effect.  We were only able to see about 5 butterflies and no butterfly clusters :(.  I think global warming is affecting these beauties.  It is still a nice place to sit on a fallen tree and enjoy majestic forest feel, and with all the eucalyptus trees around you, you are able to breath better too :).

This is such a beautifully peaceful place. Makes for a great day trip. Lots of butterflies to admire in certain areas. The trail and fields are gorgeous. Lots of room to roam and even have a picnic. Besides seeing a few dead butterflies getting pulled apart by stupid children it was a nice experience.
This place is a wonder! So beautiful and special, especially when the butterflies are active... a great walk to enjoy with your kids. A little tricky to find the first time, but so worth it.

One of the best places to visit in Santa Barbara.  Great for all ages, and anybody that appreciates nature, animals, or the beauty of butterflies!  Yes, that sounded corny... but you will not regret it!  You can easily walk to the beach and have a picnic before or after.  Sometimes there are park rangers there with binoculars and information to share, but you can bring binoculars just in case.  Cameras and pictures do not capture the beauty of this place, so definitely go and see it for yourself.  I believe the best time of year to go is late January/February?  I've gone every year at this time since I moved to Santa Barbara four years ago, and I'm amazed every time!

This place is beautiful and definitely a must see in SB.

We went the last weekend of February at around 2:00pm and there were still lots of butterflies.  There were so many resting in the trees we thought they were leaves. A few will fly around and you can see them up close and personal when they are mating and happened to fall out of the trees.

The preserve is close to the shoreline and nice little hikes are nearby.

I live down the street and walk my dog here a few times a week. The butterflies are here in the thousands every year and the best time to see them is between December and March on a sunny day so that they are active. There's nothing like it seeing so many of them just flying between the Eucalptus trees. The corridor of trees alongside the creek is one of my favorite parts of this area. It is so serene and at night you can hear and sometimes see owls above you. 

Once out on the bluffs there are many trails, 2 of which will lead down to the beach. Looking down from the bluffs you can see Coal Oil Point on one end with surfers galore and on the other end you can see the cormorant nesting pillars. Don't wear or bring anything fancy to the beach because tar can get on everything but if it does you can always rub some baby oil on it and it should come off. 

Great place to go birding, walk your dog (there may be some dogs off leash even though they aren't technically supposed to be), go running, mountain bike, and consider how great life can be. I never leave feeling unhappy.
The best FREE thing to do in Santa Barbara!!!

Just lovely!  Parents, bring your kids here.  Men and women, bring your significant other here.  Heck, everyone take some time to check it out!

Hiking the path to the woods nearby is a refreshing trek, with the reward of two of nature's best shows.  Butterflies and, if you come at dusk, beautiful sunsets.

There really is a mucho number of butterflies at the Butterfly Preserve, and the people who are watching alongside you are often just as interesting as the sight of hundreds of fluttering wings.  Clustered high in the branches of the trees in the forest, tons of butterflies float around.  At first you have to look close as they look like leaves.  Careful inspection reveals they are everywhere.  We're surrounded.

Be sure to walk a little further to the cliffs edge for a breathtaking view of the ocean, and one of the loveliest sunsets Mother Earth can offer.

AWESOME!  So many different trails to follow.  Not as many butterflies as we would have liked to see.  The weather was PERFECT, and the company was even better!  We walked in far enough to walk to the little cliff to look down at the ocean.  Spending Easter here was amazing, peaceful, beautiful, and I hope it can become a family tradition!!

Emerson once said that one should never lose an opportunity to see anything that is beautiful. This is especially true of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve and Ellwood Beach. In fact, this place is so stunning, so overwhelmingly beautiful that describing it in words would seem to diminish the experience entirely.

I think I fell in love this weekend--if only because this place is the stuff of romantic poetry. A butterfly grove, a grassy pasture filled with brilliant yellow mustard flowers, bluffs overlooking thirty miles of pristine coastline, the warm 4 o'clock sun and the scent of sweet eucalyptus in the air. Distilled down, it offers something for which I seem to be endlessly searching: perspective. 

The Saturday afternoon I spent here with my best friend--to be filed under: Yes.

This place is GORGEOUS. we came here on sat morning and the butterflies were so alive! We went through the forest looking place and then a short hike to the beach area. the beach was SO PRETTY!! We were near ellwood. Bring sandals or flip flops if you don't wanna ruin your tennis shoes from the sand! Lots of people biked to the beach and or walked! We walked and got to see a pretty nice view of the beach! There were alot of butterflies at this time! So go!

Great place for easy hike/walk. Bring your binos or camera with a long lens to get the best view. Only reason It's not a 5 star is because there used to be 10x's the amount of butterfly's 20 years ago : (  jan is in my opinion the best time to go, around noon because they are way more active when it's warm.

We arrived a week after a heavy storm, so not sure if that affected the monarch groupings in the trees, but it was nice nonetheless. My bf and I didn't know what to expect. We just searched for something cheap and close to do. 

Upon arriving, we parked in the parking lot and followed a couple people through the trails. They are marked but there are so many trails we were playing it safe. 

We walked through the trees, behind the residential homes. We couldn't see any ocean views. Then it took a turn and we walked upwards, splitting away from the people in front of us. Once on top, as we broke through the tree area, we could hear water breaking. It opens up into a large field area. Then you keep walking and ta-da.... cliffs overhanging the beach. Absolutely beautiful. 

Along the entire walk, we saw a few butterflies. So it was worth the trip. I would like to return better prepared with proper shoes and walk a little further. 

If you are looking for a little nature walk, I highly recommend this place.
A great place for all ages but my preschool grand daughters really like seeing the butterflys.

Great hiking trails that go all the way to the cliffs overlooking the ocean.  Dog friendly  - which is great.  Lots of people with dogs and evidence suggested they picked up after their furry friends!!  The Eucalyptus grove which is off to the left when you walk in is the butterfly habitat.  I wasn't expecting to see any butterflies since they said the season was Oct-Feb.  We did actually see a couple dozen stragglers here and there, so that was bonus. Will have to make it back during butterfly season!!

Hundreds of Thousands.....literally!  HUNDREDS OF THOUSANDS of butterflies adorned trees, logs, streams, the air....you name it, they adorned it

Everywhere - just everywhere you looked, there were butterflies.  The Park Ranger said they estimated around 200,000 were there this yr & it was a low-number year.....W - O - W

Each year, Monarch Butterflies migrate up the CA coast & for some reason, they absolutely love this little forest cove.  But in all reality, I can totally see why - it's absolutely gorgeous!

Located smack-dab in the middle of a neighborhood, if you didn't know what you were looking for, you'd never know it was here

Enter the Preserve & walk along the trail for only a short moment before you come upon Ellwood Grove.....which are the trees that house the thousands of butterflies

Continue walking along the trail & come upon the most beautiful coastal scenery.  This would be a great spot to picnic or watch the sunset.  We also saw lots & lots of walkers & joggers & this trail fit the need nicely

Butterflies are only around Dec till March so come in the winter to see the migration or come anytime to enjoy beautiful trails along the coast.....it's a win-win!

This would *definitely* qualify for a repeat, yearly visit!

Best Tip:  Bring a proper camera.  I only had my phone & the pics didn't do justice to our experience!
Tens of thousands of butterflies, what else can I say?  Even without the great hiking trails nearby that dance along the bluffs overlooking the ocean, this place would still get 5 stars.

This place was great, just beautiful! And for UCSB students, there is a bus that goes to ellwood, well close! bus 25! lol this place is really worth coming, its great, its beautiful. I can't believe this place exists so close to us.
No butterflies :( I came Nov 24 in the afternoon, thinking it would be peak season. Most of the trails were closed, so we only spent 5 minutes walking around.
Amazing spot. Butterflies as far as the eye can see. When it's not butterfly season, it's still a fantastic spot for a walk/run.

Even in February you can enjoy great weather and great hiking!  From butterflies to birds...you will see so much!  I strongly recommend this for anyone looking for a 'gentle' walk as the trails are easy on the knees.  With just a few areas changing grade, it's kid friendly as well as senior friendly.

If you decide to go to the beach....which I strongly recommend, it's very flat and firm. During the week there are very few people around, so photo opportunities are endless.

Enjoy!

Of course it's free and so is the parking!

Great hike!
Very nice escape. There were lots of Butterflies but they were all grouped and not flying.

Enchanting. Magical. Sublime.
These are three adjectives that come to my mind when describing this place.

As others have reviewed, this is where THOUSANDS, yes, THOUSANDS of monarchs come to roost in eucalyptus trees during the late fall and winter months. 

The location of this preserve is nestled within a suburban neighborhood on Coronado Dr. Street parking is usually available. If you have a hard time finding a parking space here, you might want to head over to the Goleta Butterfly Grove, where they have a huge free parking lot (located a few blocks north on Hollister).

It's probably good to know that this preserve is FREE! However, it would be awfully kind if you made a donation. :) They have a donation box at the beginning of the preserve, along with an explanation about the preserve itself. 

Begin walking along a dirt path, and you will find yourself immersed in a native coastal sage scrub habitat. Immediately, you will begin to hear the sounds of hummingbirds and other types of birds. Yes. This preserve is known for its monarchs, but where monarchs abound, so do birds! 

Continue walking along the trail and you will be surrounded by hundreds of eucalyptus trees. On windy days, these trees creak a lot. Do be careful, since eucalyptus tree branches are known to fall down and possibly cause injury. Soon, you will see butterfly signs guiding you to spots for the best viewing of the monarchs. 

To make the most of your experience here, bring/wear the following:
1.) a good pair of binoculars, 2.) a good camera, 3.) durable footwear such as lightweight hiking boots/sneakers with good treading, 4.) layers of clothing--it gets windy here, but can be warm once out in the open (don't forget sunglasses).

Once you get to the spots where the monarchs congregate, be ready to be amazed!  Truly. When the timing is right, you will be astounded. 

Thousands of monarchs fly about up in the tree-line area, where they find a place to roost and just bask in the sun--when there is sunlight. 

After hanging out here for a bit, you might want to continue on the various trails that meander into the fields that lead to the bluffs that overlook the ocean. It's so nice, especially since this area is undeveloped. It's so pristine, as it should be. And if you have eagle eyes like me, you will see so much. :) As mentioned in the website for The Land Trust for Santa Barbara County, "The Preserve is protected open space and acts as a gateway to existing and future protected coastal resource areas nearby, via trails linking the Goleta Monarch Grove, Ellwood Shores coastal bluffs, Santa Barbara Shores County Park, the Devereux Slough and the beach." Good to know!

I know there were some complaints about not seeing more monarchs. Well, we can blame ourselves for that. Numbers have been declining due to climate change and destruction of habitats. Thank God for places such as these, that ensure these lovely creatures to thrive.

Very nice and peaceful area and there are many butterflies from November. It's a short hike to the Ellwood Cove where all the butterflies are.  Make sure you have a high zoom camera or binoculars to enjoy them, since you can't get too close since it is roped off. If you continue to walk past the cove it will lead you out to a cliff overlooking the ocean. We walked further to the left and walked down to the beach. Be careful of the steep decline down. We had a fun full day enjoying the butterflies and the beach below.

It's not bad for a little side trip if you're in the area and it's the right season for the butterflies.  Heck, even when it's not the right season for the butterflies, it's a pretty little walk. Not a hard hike, suitable for children as long as you're making sure they don't fall into the water.

Unfortunately, as of July 2017, all the trails are currently closed in the preserve because most of the trees are dead due to the drought and the city decided it was too dangerous to have open at this time. So, if you go, be aware that you're going at your own risk and face possible fines.

The city is working on removing the dead trees that pose safety hazards so the preserve should be able to open up again next year. More info about the work to re-open the preserve is here: http://www.cityofgoleta.org/projects-programs/ellwood-mesa-habitat-project

This place is absolutely spectacular. The best time to visit is between November- January. There are so many monarchs and they kinda blend into the trees. Gilbert the butterfly man is the docent and he knows just about everything anyone would ever want to know about everything! Three thumbs up!!
Its a park, Its clean, leads to the beach and bike trails. Pretty much great.

We were here in late December of 2014. We came to the main resting spot with thousands of butterflies resting on a few branches. They were hard to see and we probably would have missed them if we weren't looking for them. I was bummed I didn't have my telephoto lense and my slr camera. If you keep walking past the woodsy area you'll end up on this awesome ocean bluff. The view was spectacular! We will definitely be back. If I was a local this would be my primary biking/jogging spot. A quick shout out to the City of Goleta for keeping this preserve and not allowing houses to be built here!

This place is amazing!!! I actually came twice in a week (and I live in Los Angeles).  

This past week, my boyfriend and I came here around noon on a Wednesday. The sun was in the right place and the butterflies were in full flight...so beautiful!  

After watching the butterflies in the trees walk up the hill and out of the foresty area.  If you follow the path you end up on a cliff overlooking the ocean.  We brought a blanket and a picnic and enjoyed the crashing waves as we snacked and sunbathed (yes, thank you California for allowing me to wear a bikini in January).  

If you have a dog, bring him/her!  Tons of people were running on the path with their pets and others walked down to the beach  and let their dogs enjoy the water.  

We went for a nice hour and a half walk along the paths and down on the beach.  Such a peaceful and romantic place.  Pretty sure this will be on our list of places to come back to time and time again.
Well we didn't see any butterflies, they the area is simply stunning.  Quiet place with animals and nature.  Excellent place to just get out and take a little hike.  Very cool place.

We got there too late in the year to see most of the butterflies, but it was an amazing trip regardless - a nice walk, gorgeous views, and enough butterflies fitting in and out of the sunlight to give this little treasure of a preserve a magical feeling for my family and I...  We will definitely be going back earlier in the "season" for the full effect.

This place is a favorite of ours when we are just looking for something to do and want to be out in the fresh air, checking out the beauty of nature. The butterfly season is November through February and it is an amazing, beautiful experience. We have gone out of season though and enjoyed ourselves just as much! It is a nice little walk through the Eucalyptus trees and then you are out in the open walking toward the cliff overlooking the ocean.

There are MANY photo ops here; I highly recommend sunset off the cliff... breathtaking views! Don't worry if you don't want to take a real hike, this is more of a walk (though you will need to "hike" up a hill to get through the tree area where the butterflies (and owls) are. Boys, I think you need to take your special ladies here because they will definitely love you for it!

Definitely make a trip when the butterflies are there. Imagine a forrest of beautiful eucalyptus trees with what seems to be a million monarch butterflies flying around. The Monarchs start arriving as early as October but the best time to check it out is December through February. Take your camera.
Beautiful place to take a morning or afternoon walk. I have yet to see a butterfly and I have yet to care.

What a wonderful find!  The signs were a little misleading and it was initially hard to find - seems like the neighbors don't like all the butterfly admirers which flock to this area during mating season.  There are lots of butterflies fluttering about, and it really is a unique experience.  The sky got a little dark as they flew overhead and blocked the skylight, and it was  magical to see them hanging and creating clusters on the eucalyptus trees.  It was my first time going to a butterfly preserve, and so glad we were able to see them on their migratory journey. Peak times to see the Monarch butterfly range from December through February; though they begin arriving as early as October and are usually gone by the end of March.
Had a good time showing the butterflies to my two-year-old son.  Nice simple trails and a couple fun, small bridges.

Visited in the morning today. This place is about 13 miles north of Santa Barbara near I-101.  We parked on the residential street on Coronado Dr.

Saw only a few lonely butterflies. Walked through the eucalyptus tree forest toward the ocean. I smelled the scent of the fresh eucalyptus trees. Saw groups of wild yellow flowers. 

The bees were busy hanging around the flowers under the warm sun. The birds sang to us as we walked along. The only thing that bothered me was the airplane noise.

Some trails have fallen tree so we crawled over/under them. The trails are still children friendly. Expect some muddy spots.
It was such a beautiful baby blue sky. 
Will have to monitor other posts for butterfly sightings.
It was a nice walk, some of the trails are closed. I looked high and low, but there were not many butterflies here. I counted 10 flapping around but I did not see any groups of them in the trees.

Recently a co-worker told me about this place, so I came up with my fiance the Friday after Thanksgiving.  We didn't leave OC until noon, and because of some traffic, we didn't get here until after 3PM.  Does not leave a lot of light left in the eucalyptus grove but I was able to get some nice shots all the same. There was a gentleman there (docent probably) answering questions.  We ventured out on the bluff and walked around until sunset and took a few more pics.  Walking back in almost dark had a creepy vibe, with owls hooting and other strange sounding birds... LOVED IT!.  The trails are not at all hard to manage but I still got a nice workout all the same.  I will definitely be back, and earlier in the day so I can get more butterfly photos to share.
even when the butterflies are not here, it is a beautiful place to walk. The fragrance of eucalyptus is truly salubrious. Wander out to the Ellwood Bluffs and take in the fantastic views.

This was very neat.  I recommend parking over by the residential neighborhood to the northeast of the preserve rather than the official entrance.  There is another entrance over there and it's a shorter walk.

Highly recommended for nature lovers!  My daughter and I visited on Sunday, 1/19, to see the monarch butterflies.  There were two locations where they could be found, and at first, I saw a few flitting around and was a little disappointed until my 8 year old pointed out to me that they were all hanging from the trees, looking just like leaves (the signs tell you this, but I had not paid attention).  Wow, it was a really cool sight to see, hundreds of butterflies grouped together, dangling from branches.  The hike through the park was also really pretty.  Free parking, free views, how can you beat it...about a 5 minute drive away was shops and restaurants and then Santa Barbara is about 15 minutes south.  It makes for a very nice, scenic visit.

I went on valentine's day with some friends and there were still a ton of butterflies. It was cloudy so they were mostly all in the trees resting but I hear when the sun is out there are literally clouds of them flying all around you. Not a serious hike by any means. Make sure you wander beyond the main grove as just over the hill is a beautiful view if the ocean from atop a cliff. You can then hike down the cliff to the water for some sweet tidepooling.

This trail is a fun place to hike for families! The trails are wide, no steep hills, plenty of shade, and leads to the ocean. The best part were the Monarch butterflies. It was fun to see them for a few minutes, but got boring. However, the kids loved chasing them for quite some time. My party arrived on a Saturday morning and there weren't too many people hiking and there were signs stating that the trails were closed due to unsafe paths, which concerned us, but we didn't want to turn around as we were looking for a hike that morning! So, be warned that they officially close at any given moment. Also, the grass looked dead and would have been awesome if they were still green! Once you get out of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve, you will get to the Ocean which is always a beautiful sight to see! Great place if you're looking for a quick hike!

I never knew I was such a voyeur.   But this place is entomological pornography at its finest.

Nestled at the end of Coronado Drive in Goleta (from Hollister Ave., turn south onto Coronado Drive and you'll see it at the end of the street, on the right), the trail head to the Coronado Butterfly Preserve is deceptive.  After finding plenty of parking on the surrounding streets, and trekking up the first hill I was wondering when we'd see butterflies.  The trail is an easy stroll and we saw many toddlers managing the walk with their parents just fine.
