I wouldn't go out of my way to visit the refuge and I don't know many locals who have even ever stopped to visit. But I like going there on occasion to have lunch on one of the benches and look at hummingbirds.

I used to live very close to the bird refuge and walk my pup along the outskirts walking path every few days. I liked it alright because there is usually a lot of activity, things to look at, and people to say hi to. But the stinky stench and the grimey mud got to be a little too much. 

The resting place, where you can park and sit on a bench, use the stretching machines, or stand and watch the birds fly in over the slimy toxic looking bay is really nothing too amazing and definitely not a recommend "tourist attraction." During the summer it can be pretty beautiful to just get off Carrillo and drive around to East Beach because the sun reflecting off the water with the palm background is a sight to see, but no need to stop and see the bay or the birds.. You can see plenty of birds at East Beach right across the street. 

I would love to see some sort of restoration done, maybe by a bird rescue non-profit(?), to put some effort into making the bay less toxic looking and stinky. Maybe have them clean up the greenery as well and put in some less odorous plants that don't need as much water supply - Kill two birds with one stone, not literally of course.

Tranquility.

I appreciate the refuge on most days. Other days I imagine how great this place could be with a large development.

I've been meaning to Meander this bird refuge for sometime and today was the day.  Parking was simple, then my pup and I headed down the paved road to see what birds  where out and about.  It's a pleasant stroll, lined on either side by flora and trees and varying species.  Many ducks and rare and beautiful bird types are in the lake  and along the grassy knolls. Stranger Things style, a group of pre-teen kids whizzed by us on bikes.  This is a great option if you'd like to get in a chill, light workout.  And the lake closes at 10pm, which a great option when the tides run high on the beach and it's much too dark to hike.  And it basically falls around the bend from where northern Coast Village Road ends. FYI: just saw a post on Nextdoor w/neighbors, car break-ins+stolen credit cards happened today @@11am.  Don't bring yr wallet.

Not the cleanest of spots, but still a nice place to visit. 

To put it politely, the Bird Refuge is very "natural." It doesn't appear to have been improved or enhanced with man-made conveniences, but does offer a glimpse into unobstructed nature. It wraps around the eastern end of Cabrillo Blvd. and Los Patos Way and can be seen from the road. There's a walking path that lines most of the perimeter, and there are a few benches and docks for your viewing pleasure. Los Patos is somewhat awkward as it juts from the freeway, but you'll be able to find street parking nearby. 

As other reviewers have stated, the body of water at the center of the park is dirty and gives off an unpleasant odor. I suppose that this is how you'd find it in a natural setting so in theory, it doesn't bother me. I've seen more ducks than traditional birds congregate here. Maybe I'm just coming on the wrong days. 

Overall, a decent spot certainly worth a visit if you happen to be walking along the Cabrillo path.

name: U-Haul Storage of Santa Barbara
categories: Movers, Self Storage, Trailer Rental, Propane, Automotive, Home Services, Truck Rental, Local Services

yelp_reviews.text:
I have been storing here for almost a year. I often leave messages for Stacy the manager, that take weeks to return. 

I chose a unit that had elevator access. However, the elevator has been broken for over 3 months now and they say they have tried to repair but they can't get the parts. I was given a discount for 1 month of $20. 

When I asked again about the elevator situation, I was told that it was not going to be fixed and that I can't get a discount again. The young man also said lucky to have the unit I have. (Really?) He then told me that they were going to remove the elevator from the other building. 

I am very upset about this since I specifically chose this place because I could put my stuff on an elevator instead of going up and down stairs. I am a middle aged woman. 

In addition, when I went to get some of stuff out of my unit, 3 weeks ago, all my stuff in the front of the unit was covered in saw dust. They had just repaired the floor outside my unit and must've have cut the boards right there. Needless to say, this is not what I would want or expect.

I am waiting now for 3 weeks for Stacy to return my calls. I have left multiple messages.

I am looking into renting from another business in town.

Follow-up: at 1:30pm today Stacy finally called me. She was defensive and said she tried calling me several times and left messages. I did not have any voice mails and only 1 missed call from last week. She kept saying she's done all she could do for me and that for me, it wasn't enough. I responded by saying, that it seems like all you want to do for me is tell you can't do anything for me.

She didn't apologize for the sawdust in my unit, for the attitude of her staff (Enrique). She is a nice person, I believe, but she does NOT know how to run a business or treat her customers.

She did not even TRY to address 1 of my concerns. All she wanted to do was be defensive and make excuses. I will be moving out this week.

DO NOT RENT STORAGE FROM THIS PLACE!

Update to previous comments:

9/19/16

I did not move out because they did finally fix the elevator and they gave me a $20 discount for a few months for my troubles. They have put in new floors and painted everything as well. I still think they are a bit inept but I'm staying for now. They have recently raised my rent but just a little, so I'm not highly motivated to get my things out of there.

For renting a van/truck they are horrible!!!!!! We were bounced around 2 different locations and encountered rude service and lack of commitment to our "reservation" we had made a week prior. The worker at the third location was no where to be found when we arrived, and when we called he aggressively said he would be 30mins to an hour late. Absurd. At the second uhual location we were sent to, they said reservations were "first come first serve red based on availability"...how is that a reservation??? So disappointed. We were supposed to be moving our stuff starting at 9 am and by almost 11 we still didn't have a truck or van. Get it together.

I just moved out after having my stuff there for about three years. As Storage facilities go, this is the best I've ever had. 
While I was there the price did increase once and they gave me proper notice.
They were always very friendly and helpful, and most importantly - they didn't nickel and dime me with fees. 
Thanks guys!

WORST experience ever here - they charged us for a "scratch" which nobody would ever even mark as damage in a walk through - don't rent here - you will pay deductible- the lady who dropped off a truck right before us also got slammed for "damage". This place is definitely dodgy. It's a shame because I really love the uhaul service but I will never rent one again
Buyer beware! Sold me a smaller locker than advertised (I can't help the way its listed sir) and lost original reservation.

Avoid this place if you can! I needed to rent a moving truck after my grandmother passed to transfer her belongings. I called and asked how much because it was a two city stop and the girl on the phone said she could give us a $70 discount to cover the cost of the extra miles. She said to come in the day of and that we had made a reservation only call if we needed to make a change. Got the email thought everything was set in stone. 

The day of they couldn't find my reservation I showed them the email and was told that it was just a quote. I tried to explain the situation, that the lady I spoke to didn't mention this. 

I was very upset and asked to speak to the manager but of course I was already talking to the manager. I said I was told this price and I have the email with the price given she continued to say "I can't give you that price" so I said just forget it I need the truck I have no other option. Her tone was extremely rude. 

BUT what gets this negative review is the fact that she was talking on the phone right in front of me about the situation to the other store manager down the street laughing and saying "I don't know how she got that idea" "I know I know" 

Terrible customer service it's just not worth it specially if you are in stressful situation like I was.

I would give this place zero stars if I could. Avoid this place at all costs. We reserved a unit two months before needed, and on the morning we were to arrive at the facility with a truck full of furniture, they called us to tell us they did not have our space available. AND THAT WAS IT. They said they were sorry but there was nothing they could do for us. WHAT? WHO DOES THAT? We were advised to start making phone calls to other facilities to see if someone else could take our furniture. After making calls on our own, we found a place that could take our stuff that day. Unfortunately, it was more expensive. We called back this storage place and told them we were able to find another place, but at a higher rate and asked them to credit us for the difference for all the troubles. I don't know why I was surprised when they said no, but they did. After several phone calls over the next week, demanding for some sort of compensation for our additional expense, AT THEIR FAULT, we finally got our credit. Keep in mind, we were not asking for anything for free, for all our troubles, just asking for the additional costs we incurred at their hands, to be rectified. 

I am in the service industry, and I do understand that things happen that are beyond our control, but the fact that their original call that morning was to say that they didn't have the unit we reserved, as planned, and that we were basically on our own, and didn't really try to find a solution for us, is totally unacceptable.

Geez nothing fast about this place just needed a box & took 15 minutes... The employees don't try to move at any pace that actually helps this business. Don't seem like you're in a hurry either or they move slower. There shouldn't be a list of complaints for a sale of 1 box.
1) only one person in front of me & it took 15 minutes 
2) the employee kept asking the customers to close & lock the front door for her 10 minutes before closing. "I'm not staying open all night lock the door" 
3) locked the front door & said no one could leave out it anymore but not smart enough or courteous enough to say please use this other door so the customers can get on with their day too. 
4) a man came in from out back not responding to the cashier when she spoke to him then slammed the back door so loud that everyone inside commented. closing a business early because you're bothered & want to go home is no way to run a Successful business. You just lost 1 customer & the guys in front of me were pretty upset as well. Just no way to treat your paying customers.

This U-Haul Storage Facility was okay.  The manager/staff are friendly and nice.  Easy to work with.  As for the facility, they just repainted it so it looks new.  I never had any problems with losing items or any security issues.  The car isles are a little small and you have to be a careful driver.  Plus the hours of operation are only till 7pm some nights.

If you want a good cheap storage facility, I would go with this one, but read your contract and ask questions about charges.  One thing I got frustrated with is, I check out 1-2 days after my payment date.  The charge was still pending on my credit card and they would not prorate my storage rent.  They charged me for the whole month even though I no longer have an account with them.

NEVER get a trailer hitch installed by these guys ! I bought a custom (as in "ordered and made for my specific make/model/year vehicle" ) hitch from U-Haul, planned to install it myself, but after reading very clear and explicit instructions decided the job was a bit beyond my time constraints. I brought it back to U-Haul and contracted w/ them do the installation. 

I was quite surprised to see they did not follow the installation instructions.  Those instructions detail the removal of my car's existing frame access covers, drilling bolt holes for the included hitch mounting bolts & brackets, and replacing the covers. They instead chopped open  the frame of my car to make it expedient and convenient for them to install the hitch. They also omitted installation of support brackets that came in the kit.  

I now have sections of my frame missing because the installer, rather than follow the explicit OEM installation instructions just sawed off sections of my frame to make it easy and quick, thinking I would not notice. Both the manager and installer tried to BS me that the installaion was on par with hitch installation industry standards and that the omitted bracking was not necessary. 
 
 The round holes that used to be where the access covers fit  were sawed away during this butchering, The covers themselves were not put back on. In fact, the covers were kept, along with the missing braces,  by the installer until I demanded they be given back to me. 

I subsequently brought the car home, installed the omitted bracing myself  but now I have gaping rectangular holes exposing the internal metal of the vehicle's frame where there used to be original weatherproof undercoating, and sealed rubber access covers. 

I will next take U-Haul to small claims court if they refuse to reimburse me the cost of having the fram repairedand re-undercoated to the conditioin ist was in before.

Absolutely awful. Upon making a reservation I was told the unit would be on the second floor but "not to worry" there was a freight elevator. When I arrived, after a 20min. check-in & checking that the unit was near a freight elevator, I was sent out to find my unit. Found it up a narrow, enclosed staircase, down a long hallway, NO freight elevator in the building. I went back to the office, told them no elevator-"Oh really? Hang on." I waited 20 more minutes just to be told, "Um, I think that's all we have." Great, thanks for the hour of my life I'll never get back. I took my business elsewhere.

Five stars! The address is actually 4417 Hollister ave. I went the location listed and it was not there but the correct address is just right down the street. I went to get my propane filled in my rv and was quickly greeted by Angel and an angel he was! He was very knowledgeable about what he was doing. Great customer service. When my van was a bit to low for the propane to fill he came up with a solution within seconds. He had it filled up in no time. I would reccomend this location if you are looking for fast friendly service!

Great experience today at Uhaul Storage in Santa Barbara.  We were tired and sore from moving these last weeks...three in a row.  Finally needed to put the leftover stuff in storage.  Nathaniel greeted us so warmly and professionally, we felt like family.  He quickly got us what we needed and on our way in no time at all.  Nathaniel is a great example of why we keep coming back to Uhaul for our rental and storage needs.  Uhaul, he's a keeper!  Thank you again!!!

I had a horrible experience when I returned my rental van at this location.  The employee tried to charge me for a tiny little scratch on the side of the van that was about the 2mm in diameter.  It was a total shakedown.  Really sad because it was convenient for me and the price was good but I will never go back.  The business model here is lure you in with a low advertised price then sell you the insurance or charge you for alleged damage.
Easy process, clean van, great price. Sandra went above and beyond to make our experience great. Super kind and helpful. Overall a fantastic day.

10/10 experience, The employee who was working today was extremely helpful (I believe her name is Sandra) I paid a very reasonable price to rent a moving van for 5+hrs and I had so many questions because I have never rented a moving van before, but she was extremely helpful and Answered them all very efficiently.

Truly bad. I gave them
A two star review before I was stupid enough to return but every experience I have dealing with U-Haul  is hideous and time wasting. Ridiculously bad service.

This place is absolutely awe full, therefore they rate NO STARS!!!   They have the worst customer service.  The manager Gabrielle would not talk to me in regards to reimbursement of my 3 weeks owed to me.  I called headquarters, and they apologized for the manager at the store.  They charged my credit card without my permission.  I really believe they are scammers, and am taking it to a higher level, and also the BBB.  Don't rent a storage unit at the u haul on mid State Street in Santa Barbara!!  It won't let me post unless I give a Star!!!

Agree with Emily J. 

Fantastic. 

I had to get a bed frame on Anacapa to my place in San Roque. 19.95 plus the mileage (which was under 10). 

HUGE pickup truck. When they said pickup, I was thinking some puny little Toyota Tacoma type. 

Lovely to drive. 

Great service. 

All good.

It's ok but it's very expensive to have a storage unit in Santa Barbara any beach town I would assume rent is so high in southern California it is  Enrique was nice he shook hands with
me one time and apologized for any misunderstanding I think him and his wife try to do the best they can It would be hard to manage a business children and a second job for anyone. I think the rent should go down cheaper $100 is very expensive for one unit.

name: 7 Falls Hike
formatted_address: Tunnel Rd And Mission Canyon Rd

yelp_reviews.text: A fun hike. I wish there was more water flowing. Very little water throughout the trail. The inspiration point lookout is amazing with views all the way to the ocean.

"In every walk with nature one receives far more than he seeks."  -- John Muir

"Not all that wander are lost." -- Unknown 

One of my favorite hikes in the hills above Santa Barbara.  Part of the Tunnel Trail system, you can wander to your hearts continue to and fro to the Falls and beyond...all the way to the top of Inspiration Point or, on another trail, all the way till it ends near the fire damage.  

The hike is about 2 or 3 miles round trip with an elevation gain of about 800 ft.  I prefer adding additional hikes on the surrounding trail system.  Parking at the trailhead, which is located at the end of Tunnel Rd in a congested neighborhood,  is limited and strict. You must park behind the white line or else they really do ticket and tow.  You can get to the Falls, by hiking, from the other entrance on San Roque Rd for the Jesusita Trail.

The trail is overgrown and covered with poison oak so hopping on rocks, wading thru the water and some balancing thru the creek is the best way to go. Not easy but fun and rewarding. The pools and falls are ample right now so go now to see it!

I expected a leisurely hike and had an amazing unexpected adventure!  What a beautiful hike.  I posted the full story with photos on my blog: http://www.shoestringadventures.com/2012/11/a-perfect-day-for-wild-goat-chase.html

Been hiking to this spot and 3 Pools for years and it's always been a fun hike. You can stay on  the trail or you can go off the trail and rock hop until  you reach the destination. I went this past weekend 10/16/2016 and unfortunately there was not a single drop of water to my disappointment. I remember jumping 20 feet into the pool of waters and now seven falls and 3 pools is bone dry. Hike is still fun though, but don't expect any water to swim in!

DAAAYYYUUUM! Thats what I call a hike!!

Or hike/rock-climbing/sliding down rocks kind of a hike.... And its like they say about McDonalds... "Im loving it"

We went the wrong way first and ended up at the top where we overlooked Santa Barbara... AMAZEBALLS! the view is to die for! 

As we came down again we saw some people coming from where the 7th waterfall is and we started to "hike" (read rock-climb) over there.... 

When we came to the creek and fall, we went into the water and enjoyed the cold water... 

All I can say is that I love love love this place and I am already planning a trip to Santa Barbara soon to go to 7 falls again....

Please do not attempt to go there if you are out of shape because it will kill you

Sweet hike. We have done it a few times, but today as we were coming up, about 5 minutes from the last deep hole that everyone swims in, there was a rattle snake sitting on a rock we walked past!
It was awesome, but kind of sketchy because we didnt see him until we were right next to him. He started rattling, but then we were able to just walk away. Luckily he was up on a rock and not by our feet.

Don't be intimidated by our experience though, the snake wasn't aggressive at all

This is one of our regular hikes... though I guess I'm using the term 'hike' liberally. It's mostly scrambling/climbing over rocks and wading through streams. And for my boyfriend, it's an opportunity to jump around like a mountain goat. 

There are a couple of different ways of doing this hike, one of which takes you around the mountain and you end up at the pools at falls at the top, but we prefer the gutsy route (i.e. more dangerous and fun): upstream through the water. It is really easy to get lost on this hike, so it helps to go with someone who has done this before. It also helps to be flexible, have no fear afraid to do stuff (like climbing vertical rock walls), and be prepared to get wet! This route normally takes a good 4-5 hours round trip if you make it all the way to the pools, longer if you're bringing people who have never done it before and might be hesitant. 

Depending on the time of year and how much rain we've had the pools, especially the first one at the giant waterfall can get really deep and people will line up to slide down the falls into the pool. Generally this is pretty safe if the water is high.

For pretty detailed directions getting there, Santa Barbara Hikes has a pretty awesome step-by-step guide: http://santabarbarahikes.com/hikes/frontcountry/3pools.shtml 

If you can make it up all the way past the seven falls, which are lovely and fun (especially going down), there are three very tranquil pools. This is a lovely spot to relax a bit before the excitement getting back down. My boyfriend swears up and down he's seen wild turkeys just beyond those, but no one believes him.  

Musts for this hike: water-appropriate shoes (we like Vibram five finger shoes, though sometimes sand will sneak in), leave your electronics at home!, and bring lots of water and substantial snacks
The falls are dry right now but it was a fun adventure.  We ended up doing the Inspiration Point hike as well.  What a fun way to spend a few hours in Santa Barbara.

They need better signage, let me rephrase that, they need signage period. There were no signs at all that said 7 Falls other than what someone had written in pen on one of the fire hazard signs. My husband and I gave up after the path became too confusing. I wasn't about to keep hiking to keep turning around because we went the wrong way. We probably did this 3 times total and I wasn't sure if the falls were dried out or not, so i decided it wasn't worth it. Maybe if we researched more and if there were signs leading us in the right direction, the trip would have been successful. Two stars for a mostly shaded hike. But unfortunately that's about it.

This is the best hike!! I'm only four starring it because I get lost sometimes and the rock climbing can get pretty hard in a couple spots. It is so much fun!! It feels more adventurous than other hikes because you climb up the river bed and get to rock climb. And nature is beautiful as usual. :) Definitely recommend if you're looking for things to do in Santa Barbara.

Nice hike very close to downtown Santa Barbara.  First portion of hike is a paved trail, followed by rocky, single track, with lots of switchbacks.  We opted to take the trail to inspiration point, versus the waterfalls, as we were told by other hikers, the waterfalls and pools were kinda weak in terms of flow.  One person stated one of the pools had "icky" water in it.  "Icky" (in my mind) equates to mold and mosquitoes, which I didn't feel like dealing with.  The hike to inspiration point was beautiful.  Lots of interesting plants/flowers along the way, spectacular views, and we even saw a rabbit (the only wildlife, other than birds we came across). We went on a weekday, so it wasn't crowded, however, I could see this crowded trail becoming annoying on a busy weekend.

This is a GREAT hike that is both scenic and challenging! Finding the 7 Falls really should not be challenging, but when I went last week there was a big arrow in white chalk pointing one direction when you reach a fork in the road to the right -- DO NOT follow it! That takes you to the top of the mountain, not the falls!! 

Go left for the falls. If you want to go bouldering, 7 Falls is a great place for this! My boyfriend, brother & I spent almost 3 hours (1 hour following that misleading arrow) at 7 Falls and we had a fantastic time. We had all done indoor rock climbing before, but this was the first outdoor experience! It was a challenge, but if I can do, so can you! Plus, the trail along the creek up the 7 falls are pretty overgrown and we were told there is poison oak! 

When in the SB area, I would definitely stop by 7 Falls again. We only made up about halfway but next time I'd like to see ALL 7 falls.

There was no water when we went last presidents day weekend, bummer!  We were still able to hike up part of the mountain though.  Awesome challenge and great views. Make sure to ask other hikers for directions as we almost got lost a few times

Great hike overall but the reason I'm leaving a review is because most other reviews and/or blogs were not specific enough as far as knowing which way 7 Falls  and which way Inspiration Point actually is once at the trailhead.

So we parked about a quarter mile from the closed gate (the end of the road leading to these hikes) and there is parking along the road but it is limited. There's actually a sign that says NO PARKING PAST THIS POINT and I believe it does say to park within the white line off the road. I asked several local hikers to make sure. Ok, so once you pass the iron gate, you will hike up an old paved path for about a half mile to a mile until it turns to a dirt path. Shortly you hit your first fork - stay left for inspiration point and 7 falls - not sure where the right fork takes you. Now you will shortly hit a second fork - again, stay left (right will take you up a steep short path to an overlook - not bad but inspiration point is better). 

Once you've taken the second left you will be on a great hike down to a brook at a low point - if you cross the brook and continue on, you will eventually reach inspiration point (scenic overlook of SB) however if you take a right and hike upstream, you will be moving toward 7 falls. Realize there is very little path and mostly figuring out how to climb upstream from one natural pool to the next. It's quite the hike. I recommend wearing good, tight fitting water shoes (when water levels are good) or sneakers/hiking shoes with good grip as you will have to navigate up large rocks and skip from boulder to boulder. We made most of the way but not to the farthest pool and waterfall as the hike got pretty tough. 

At this point we stopped, my girlfriend peed on a rock, and I proposed to her - true story. All in all a fun hike - round trip about 5 miles with inspiration point.

The trail begins on a paved access road that the Edison Company uses. After a mile the road turns to dirt and you follow it just a little more toward the turnoff to Inspiration Pt. Instead of going to Inspiration Pt, you turn right and slosh your way up the creek to 7 Falls. The hike is about 3 or 4 miles round trip with an elevation gain of about 800 ft.  The creek and falls are beautiful and secluded. The cold water and warm rocks are teeming with life (newts, lizards, etc). Bouldering up the creek is good fun, but be careful it can be a bit slippery/dangerous at times. If you're agile and in decent shape you'll have no trouble.

Instead of a hike, I'd like to call this a rock-jumping, branch-ducking, river-straddling, life-risking adventure. The hike was incredible, do-able for beginners but definitely a challenge. Be prepared to actually climb vertical rock walls. 

A good tip for first timers is to forget that rock climbing course you took on the rock wall at UCSB-  with the safety rope, that hot instructor who's always got our back .. you know. Here, instead of falling on nice plush matting, you fall onto rocks and into rivers. So now that you've woken up to the realities of gravity, this hike is definitely a must-do.

When you hit the creek, follow it UPSTREAM no matter how intimidating/impossible it looks. We almost turned back had it not been for a determined, helpful group of 7 year olds who pressured us to go on (haha.) 

At the top lies the 25 foot waterfall. You then have the option to slide off it (if there is enough water) or jump off it. It is quite intimidating but by the time you get up, there are so many people waiting on you that you can't back out  :D

Don't think that just because you've hit the waterfall, its over. You can actually climb up past the waterfall to hit the hot springs further up. NOTE, this entails more rock climbing skill if you put any value on your life.

Time wise, to get to the water fall and back should take about 2-3 hours. To get to the top of the mountain (crossing the creek instead of following it upstream), add another 2 hour round trip. This is also a great hike. The way up is a lot easier than trying to get down the 70 degree incline -_-

MY FAVORITE HIKE OUT OF ALL MY HIKES SO FAR (in comparison to other hikes in Santa Barbara, San Fernando Valley and LA).  

Hiking, rock climbing, natural water slides, beautiful waterfalls and springs, and the feeling of accomplishment when you get to 7 Falls (and even a higher sense of accomplishment if you make it higher/farther to 3 pools). 

People are always nice and friendly.  Some are even willing to help/guide you when climbing some rocks (ie, when you get to 7 falls, the climb between the second and third spring is the most difficult for people.  You just have to find the holes and the climb is like climbing a ladder (literally)).  There's also a man I met who I saw again when I went on this hike on a different day.  His name's Bob Smith.  He takes pictures and posts them on his flickr.  He's fun to talk to and has this goal of going on this hike x amount of times in x weeks (I forgot the numbers).  The first time I met him he taught my friends and I on how to jump into the first pool (of 7 falls).  

Please pick up after yourselves.  This place is too breathtaking to trash (last time I went was the day before my graduation and I was upset to see hot cheetos crushed on the rocks of 3 pools and empty bottles floating in the spring).  Thanks!

The Mission Canyon area at the end of Tunnel Road features several incredible hikes, 7 Falls being one of then. You start on a paved uphill road and continue for just under a mile before reaching the trail head. From here, you have many options depending on your fitness level and aversion to risk. 

Take the dirt trail up for a few minutes until you see a turn off on your left hand side (it's just labeled "trail"). Follow the trail and cross the creek below. Now the fun begins. If you continue straight on the trail just after passing the creek you'll be heading toward Inspiration Point. This 3.5 mile round trip hike peaks at 1750 feet with spectacular views of the city. Hands down my absolute favorite run in the area (just did this yesterday, in fact). Depending on your uphill speed it should take about 45 minutes.

Walk up the boulders along the creek and you'll eventually reach 7 Falls. As the name implies, quite a lovely waterfall and a VERY popular hike, as is Inspiration Point. You'll run into plenty of people on the weekend. 

If you want to go off the beaten path and prefer rougher, more challenging terrain, cross the creek, head up a few feet and look for a trail on your left hand side. You'll follow this (veering left at trail forks) all the way to Cathedral Peak which stands at about 3300 feet. The trail is primitive and steep with plenty of bouldering to keep you interested. Don't bother trying to run this as you'll be using your arms and legs to climb much of the time.  It is physically demanding and definitely requires agility.  Follow the trail to the second highest peak (Cathedral Peak) or continue just a ways to La Cumbre Peak (the highest in Santa Barbara). You'll need a few hours to do this. Tread carefully as missteps on some of the larger boulders can really cost you. There's a small book left by local hikers at the top of the peak to record your summit. I also hiked this one over the weekend and had a blast.

Fun hike but since the actual trail doesn't actually start until after a decent trek up the hill our 12/9 yr olds were getting tired after we made it a mile and a half down the trail. A lot of personally responsible folks were wearing masks as we passed each other and the views were well worth it.

I visited here on 11/8/2020. When we visited most people people parked on the residential street to the right and walked up the hill. If you're not a local and just visiting for the first time, this trail is pretty hard to find. I saw no signs for markers
For the 7 falls trail. Make sure to do your research ahead of time or ask people along the way, as this trail connects to many others. We hiked for about an hour but we eventually turned around when we ran out of time. There was no sign of water yet in the lower streams. There is a long paved road that goes for awhile before you actually get to the dirt trails and it's mostly uphill. Beautiful views! Make sure you have enough time to finish the trails.

Took my kids age 5,6,11 on this hike. The creek was dry because no rain recently and large boulders which were hard for the kids and a lot of poison oak so beware. The kids enjoyed playing on the boulders. Worth a visit but may have to wait for a recent rain if you wanna see falls

Parking along the Tunnel Road was packed in the afternoon.  I asked some hikers along the way to confirm if we were on the correct trail since there were no signs.  Most people were going to Inspiration Point, there were only one other family from Canada that we shared 7 Falls with.  They also had a mom in there group so I figure I can do this.  We came in end of Dec so the creek was mostly dried.  But the bouldering was technical and adventurous fun.  Would like to come back with my Keen shoes when there are pools of water.  I thank God that I am still able to share these activities and memories with my family.

Lovely place to view wildlife. Home to some elusive animals. On behalf of the wildlife I ask that when swimming wear non toxic sunscreen and watch out for breeding newts. Spring is the season for mating amphibians and they hang out in the pools where people often swim. Please also respect the aquatic two stripes garter snakes that often hang out in the pools. I'm writing this because not all wildlife is respected, as recently a beheaded mountain kingsnake was discovered here, one of the most elusive animals in SB county, likely killed by a human. Please leave the wildlife be and enjoy the views!

THIS TRAIL IS CLOSED DUE TO CONSTRUCTION THROUGH 10-31-20.

The beginning of this trail starts at the same place where there is massive construction going on at Tunnel Rd.   We drove up there on October 11th, 2020, and road construction crew turned us around saying that access to the trail was prohibited   (we saw lots of other hikers get turned around as well).

No doubt this is probably a wonderful trail, but don't try to get there before  Nov 1 2020.
This is beautiful hike with breathtaking views of Santa Barbara! A lot of this hike is uphill so be sure to bring lots of water.

As if Jan 14, 2017, 7 Falls and 3 Pools is filled with water with lots of creekside rock hopping and legit rock climbing. It's a 2-2.5 hour round trip, moderately difficult hike with a rocky ascent through the creek, so prepared to bring a change of socks and shoes (I used $10 water shoes from Kmart).

To get there, Enter this in Google Maps to get there:
Spyglass Ridge Rd, Santa Barbara, CA 93105
------------------------------------------------
When you're on Mission Canyon Road, you'll have to park on the side street. There's no public parking, and be sure to be within the curb to not get towed. 

Once you're up there, go right of the water tower and KEEP HEADING on the FIRST LEFT at every fork on the trail until you have to cross the 2nd creek (1st one is over a wooden bridge). Once at the creek, turn right and walk along or in the creek until you get to the first pool. There's lots of poison oak on the trail so just avoid everything green :)

The place is cool, to get to the 2nd pool climb on the right, there's some legit rock climbing up higher up at the 2nd big waterfall. It's deep enough to jump into the 2nd pool (not sure if the 1st pool is deep enough), and it's just a refreshing spot to hang after walking through cold water. 

Leave your electronics at the base. I saw a dude had to throw his phone and shirt across the 2nd pool to a friend because there's no easy way down from the 2nd pool jump spot without going in the water. And he threw his shirt into the deep end of the first pool -_-

Hot Take: La Cumbre Peak is home to the best sunset I've seen on the west coast.

The 7 Falls Hike is enjoyable, but you really have to take in the splendor of Santa Barbara, in all its glory, on the top of the Peak. There are some picnic tables and benches up there as well, and it's (fortunately) not very crowded at the moment. If you can get up there an hour before sundown, you'll have yourself a heck of a time.

One tip: there's no gas within like 20 miles of here, so make sure you tank up before you roll up this mountain!

breathtaking. Literally and metaphorically. Lol.

It was a nice hike, specially with the cool weather and the clouds low. It was nice to get away from the cit and just take a deep nice breath and fill my lungs with cool air. The views were spectacular.

I loved how it's not that popular to a lot of people, the greenery is still very organic and natural in a sense. Wildlife roams around the area unbeknownst to human people. The mountains magnificent in their own right.

Years ago, I've hiked Seven Falls and I clearly remembered how easily it was to get lost in the trails here.  I was fortunate enough to find local hikers along the way to assist with directing me to the water pools. I vaguely recalled the trail BUT I could never forget about those beautiful, unique swimming pools which took a little bit of bouldering to get up to. It was worth every muscle in my bone to get up there... 

Recently, I've been around town for a business trip and was reminded about this trail that I've visited years and years ago. But this time around, I made sure to come fully equipped with knowledge to avoid getting lost again. So here's the skinny about this hiking path...there are TWO parts to this hike. One that leads to a scenic lookout point with views of SB and the Channel Islands (called the Inspiration Point) and the other path called "Seven Falls." Both trails are doable altogether if you have some spare time. Inspiration Point is about 3.5 miles RT and Seven Falls is about 2.5 miles RT. I personally, prefer the Seven Falls hike over Inspiration Point because being an LA native, we have tons of trails with similar scenic views of the city so Inspiration Point didn't really appeal to me.  But let me tell you about Seven Falls...this is the trail to do if you're looking for something unique, adventurous, moderate and something that's picturesque.  

Directions to 7 falls: As you walk along the paved road after about a mile in, the road turns to dirt. Keep walking ahead until you reach the turnoff to Inspiration Pt. Instead of following the above to Inspiration Point, turn RIGHT and head up to a very steep trail that takes you above 7 falls and back down to a creek again. Due to this current drought, you might not be able to see the abundance of water like years ago but the bouldering aspect of this hike is still worth the visit. I personally think hiking involving some bouldering makes for an adventure. After all, life is about seeking an adventure. The name of 7 falls meant there are 7 deep series of pools but recently, you'll notice because its DRY, you'll only see a few pools. Also to note, there's a man-made rope attached to a rock where you can use to assist yourself for climbing higher. I thought that was quite fun and endearing. =D

Parking - quite limited. I just parked along the side near the residential neighborhood along a dirt road. Good luck with parking, can't really give you any tips there.

Great hike. Don't take it literally and fall 7 times like I did. I kid, I kid. 

7 Falls Hike is fairly challening depending on who you're with as well as your own physical abilities. It helps to have people who have done it before otherwise you're stuck trying to figure out the best approach at any given time. If you're out of shape you may require some help. Generally speaking this is pretty safe though.

The water is gone mostly so it's not that nice anymore but still makes for a fun journey.

(2/22/17) : after all the rain fall the river is flowing and all of the pools are full! The water is pretty cold but if you make it to the top it's refreshing to jump in as the water is so clean and clear! 

The past couple times I have done this hike the pools and river were dry and what water was there seemed toxic. 

This time the lush greenery, crystal clear waters and open blue skies made this hike definitely one to repeat! 

PS: saw bats flying around and getting really close to us, in the middle of the day...

Seven falls/ 3 pools hike is my favorite SB hike by far! HOWEVER, I went up there yesterday 10-30-2016 and the trail gas been completely blocked off by burnt down trees. Moreover, huge boulders were falling off the mountain peaks as I crawled and climbed my way around the wreckage of trees and branches.

PLEASE message me if you have observed otherwise.. Maybe I lost my way up there and took a wrong turn?! But I am pretty sure it is not possible to go up there without a machete or chainsaw lol.

I didn't make it to the falls because a boulder the size of a kitchen stove fell out of the mountain peak and landed 30 feet away from me after I had ignored the little ones for a couple of miles, this was the "straw" that broke the camel's back.

Seven Falls is located along Mission Creek, the same creek that once provided water to the nearby Santa Barbara mission. If you visit from Dec to May you will see 7 little distinct waterfalls, all flowing into small pools, directly one after another. The waterfalls have been attracting visitors since the 1800s. 

The trail to reach the falls is about 3-miles out-and-back. Navigating the different trails can be a little tricky and involves multiple trails and unsigned forks so you may want to use AllTrails app as your guide. 

The first mile you'll walk along an uphill paved road with some really nice ocean views. After you pass a bridge and then a trail kiosk you'll take a left at the fork. After passing Mission Creek you'll take another left up a steep and rocky incline. Eventually you'll reach the point where you'll need to take a sharp right and head downhill to the canyon floor. You can use the assistance of a rope that's attached to a tree. Climbing up the first waterfall is pretty easy but the next 6 should only be attempted with caution. 

It's best to visit from Dec to May, preferably after a good rain. It's a popular destination so it's best to visit early on a weekday. Parking is limited.

Just talked to a construction worker doing construction from October to November. He said they're moving shrubs and fixing the rocks. He said it's open weekends and holidays only and they'll be out of there if not by thanksgiving then by the end of the month!

This is my to go to hike after work, before the gym, or just to relax. There's many trails it veers off to, inspiration point, cathedral peak, seven falls, and Jesusita trail. Inspiration point is to the left when you hit the creek. It's the shortest, easiest, shady, and most popular since you can see all of sb from the end of the trail. Seven falls to the right when you hit the creek follow the creek uphill. It's shady and has the natural pools (hence seven falls) including the one at the end with the water slide like rock. Cathedral peak starting point is between inspiration and seven at the creek. It's pretty long, vigorous, that goes straight up the mountain of white rocks you see as you're hiking up seven falls (I've done it twice. Takes a few hours and there's a cave towards the top where you can fit like 2-3 buses!) jesusita trail is the trail I take that shoots to the right when the pavement ends. It has no trees, it's open to get the most sun . That one seems to have no end.

I wish there were more hikes around like this one! The map actually shows an incorrect starting point...you need to take Tunnel Rd all the way up to the cross street Spyglass Ridge Rd. & Owl Ridge Rd. It's all side street dirt parking & it can get packed...so you may need to park a ways before the trailhead. 
Starts off on a paved road with a gorgeous view of the city to the ocean! There's a lot of uphill...your legs & glutes will get a good workout! Once you get down to the stream, the easy trail ends & you'll need to climb over and around some rocks & boulders to get to the end of the 7 waterfalls. It would be best to wear shoes that can get wet or take off. And careful to avoid the poison oak...there's a lot out there. 
The last waterfall has a pretty deep pool where a group of guys actually plunged into from the rocks above. And for the extreme & experienced rock climbers, there is an area just to the right of that last fall where my boyfriend was able to climb up all the way under that top rock cliff. Without any equipment or help...and only in his Converse...lol! I just watched...hoping he'd make it down safe...and he did...yay! Overall this hike took between 2-3 couple hours & it was an awesomely amazing hike...will definitely come out here again next time I'm in town:D

Very cool hike.

Here is what I remember -

There is really no parking lot to speak of so you will be trying to find a spot to park on the street in a residential neighborhood.  Plan on a pre-hike to get to the trail!

You take the middle road from the street up to the beginning of the trail which starts off on a paved road that winds up.  

After crossing a bridge, you get to a Y and begin hiking on dirt.  I'm not sure where going to the right leads.  I chose the one on the left, which ended up at Inspiration Point.

On the way, you cross a creek and water pool.  I suppose if there was water, there would be a small waterfall.  Unfortunately there was none and no salamanders to be found.  :(

The trail is deceivingly steep and rocky in spots, but overall very manageable.

Round trip from the parked car was about 5 miles.

I would definitely recommend hiking shoes and wearing layers.  It gets cold and damp in areas, but hot in others.  Sunscreen and plenty of water is also a must.

And people....PICK UP AFTER YOUR DOG.  There are bags at the entrance of the trail.  USE THEM!

I hiked this today and have to say that Cathedral Peak is an exhilarating experience and a true mental and physical challenge. Like the other Mission Canyon hikes, the terrain is gorgeous--like walking through a lesson book on the geological and botanical history of this area. There are grand sandstone formations and plenty of Manzanita trees here (one of the rarest trees in the world). The peak is just over 3300 vertical feet and you climb this in about 2-3 miles. That is to say, you gain elevation quickly, mostly on steep primitive single track trails and by climbing straight up boulders.

This trail is totally unmarked, fragmented and easy to get lost on. You'll be on a trail and then have to know to look for a vertical wall of rocks to climb instead of continuing up the path. Most people recommend doing this with someone who's hiked it before on your first attempt. Here's how to find it: begin at the end of Tunnel Road. Walk up the paved road about a mile until you get to the dirt trail head. Proceed uphill for a few minutes and take the trail junction on your left. It drops you down into mission creek. Cross the water, head right. Climb a few rocks and look for a small, steep trail on your left hand side. You take this all the way up, veering left at every junction.

Eventually the trail opens up in a bouldering area and you'll be scrambling all the way up to the peak over rocks that are sometimes 6 feet high. This hike is strenuous, but very manageable. Just be sure to bring an equally fit buddy with you. Slips are absolutely consequential and if you make a misstep, you could very well injure yourself. If you hike in shorts like me, you probably won't get through this one without some bloodshed. So prepare yourself for cuts and bruises. It's easy to sustain minor injuries and if you lose your footing on a rock, you'll be paying the price. You just can't let it happen at all! The way down is challenging too, since you're bouldering much of the way back on possibly tired legs. It's easy to get clumsy. The mental challenge here is staying sharp and focused throughout the hike.

Supply yourself with adequately tractioned shoes, plenty of water, and maybe even a few band aids. Otherwise, this is an absolutely awesome hike and you'll really feel a sense of accomplishment for completing it.

Tough tough tough, and not just saying that because I found it difficult.  It is doable, just have the right gear and know what you are doing.  

Parking: no passes required, side of the road, all the way up Tunnel Rd.
Bathrooms: none.  
Gear: gloves might be helpful but not necessary.  The correct footwear definite.
Water: ~2-4L I packed about ready for a 10 mile hike, thank God.

From the parking: take the path to the right, and then through the gates.

The trail is not as clear as other trails, and it is easy to head off onto the Inspiration trail.  The key is staying to the right at the river (dried out) crossing. The Inspiration trail will clearly go off on your left.  Just go a bit to the right and then look up the mountain and you'll see a small path sort of beaten down.  

Follow the trail up, use your god-given smarts, and you should eventually find yourself to the major boulders.  This first path up is not marked, although there are some markers left by previous hikers.  Once you are up in the boulders though, you want to keep an eye out for some red dots, and stacked rocks.  They can be faint, and they are easier seen going down than going up.  

The hike is short in length, but the amount of climbing stretches out the time significantly.  You are exposed to the elements, will get scratched, and might rip something, but its all comes with the territory, and is totally worth it.  

Happy Hiking!

Helpful: http://www.summitpost.org/cathedral-peak-santa-barbara/178817

Moved to the area a month ago from Pasadena and thought the area would be a move down in trail and peak areas and accessibility, couldn't have been more wrong.  I've climbed a lot of peaks in the San Gabriels, the Sierras, the Cascades, Colorado and all over and this is one of the most testing outside of pure rock climbing.  
Went with three guys from work and wanted a workout and some exposure to my new area, got it in spades.  Not a long hike but HARD! You will know you have done something with your day after you get done tagging this one.  Go forward and conquer...with pants and lots of hydration and a good head because you will backtrack a few times unless you are super lucky or just driven to always move forward regardless.
4-5 miles, 3 hrs+  You have been given your assignment should you choose to accept it.

Hi just want to keep it real and warn u all that this hike is not paved at all and you spend most your time just aimlessly walking up hill. which is fine and a gr8 workout with a gr8 view but when it's time to go down you're going to be like "oh no! I'm lost! search n rescue pls find me! oh no! I have no reception! help help help". then u will prob cry the entire time while finding your way back n hope n pray that u don't walk off a cliff and die. if you survive this you will prob go home and do some real research about this trail and see 99 articles of people getting lost and calling for search n rescue!! noobs beware!!

Grueling journey in the sense that it's difficult to find the correct trail but if you read around there are decent instructions online. You have to finagle your way through parts of the hike because there are breaks in the rocks that are either too difficult to summit or too narrow to climb through. Expect to get cut, it definitely happens. Once you reach the peak you'll see everything and it's really special coming back down because you realize how high up you reached.

Getting back is tricky but if you hop a few boulders you'll find a trail of some sorts. I stumbled upon a dirt trail that led eventually to the real trail. There is significant confusion after the exhaustion.

It's a good time.

We just finished up a beautiful wedding style shoot at Dos Pueblos, and I can offer my review perspective of Rancho Dos Pueblos from a wedding photographer's perspective, a perspective that might be differ a bit in nature from the other stuff you might read.

The owners are amazing to work with!  The ranch property is absolutely huge and has so many little spaces to photograph.  The ambiance feels laid back and slow, but still elegant and classy.  We chose this space for our Elegant Rustic Crystals & Lace wedding style shoot because the Ranch touches on the rustic feeling while maintaining a classy, high-end private property type ambiance.  I mean, how many places have their own private beach!  WOW!

From a photography and light perspective, the sun drops behind the north side of the canyon about 30-45 minutes before the online google published golden light times.  Not a problem to plan around, just something to be aware of when scheduling your wedding sunset photo times.  The ivy and foliage is greenest nearest the river, with a beautiful palm tree grove separating it from the iron trestles.  The rail road trestles seem like a staple, iconic Dos Pueblos Ranch landmark and are great for symmetry photographs.  The green lawn nearest the beach is a dream.  Good parking space and a perfect little beach cove to shoot in. 

What more could a photographer ask for.
Sadly, this place is showing that it is permanently closed.  It looks like the property is for sale.

This Ranch is located out of Goleta, they have private beach for weddings or gatherings also a Barn and the Casa grande as well for a nice stay. The land is beautifull surrounded by avocato trees, chirimoyas and oak and high tall eucalyptus trees.
Heaven on earth  I've spent the last 30 years with my family on this ranch & it still brings tear when we arrive. It's just breathtaking

Beautiful place for a wedding!
This huge, lush green, oceanfront property has a vintage estate to house a wedding party, a private beach, and even an upscale barn in which to hold an elegant candlelit reception.

Guests were shuttled around in buses and golf carts, so we didn't have to hike in our fancy clothes.  Narrow roads on the property are unpaved gravel, as one would expect in a secluded, thickly wooded, rustic setting. Not really a drawback, however, as a bumpy party bus ride from the main building down to the beach simply added to the fun and charm of the experience.
Ladies were advised beforehand to leave stilettos at home so all was well.

The mother of the groom (me) arrived in a helicopter that landed on a bluff just above the beach, right next to where the ceremony took place.  Best damn entrance of my life!

My son and his bride were married yesterday with the ocean as their backdrop.  Guests' eyes were glued to the sight behind the couple:  two whales frolicking in the water!
An enormous mother whale & her calf were visible just offshore during the entire time....and after vows were exchanged the whales suddenly vanished.

This magical experience left everyone speechless and breathless.  Dos Pueblos Ranch was obviously the perfect venue for an epic outdoor event.
It was a whale of a wedding in every sense of the word.

It was officially my first time of hiking in Santa Barbara.

I really enjoyed hiking here

For those of people who are looking for a date course that won't cost any money and kill some time, I highly recommend this place. You will see the beautiful vista point when you reached the top. It took me about an hour to get there.

There are plenty of street parking spaces right before the entrance.

Just feel kinda unfortunate that the lake was dried so I couldn't see the water falls - it would be so much better if I saw it so.

This spot provides a great view of Santa Barbara and the Channel Islands! I went hiking on Feb 12, 2011 and the weather was great.We took the Jesusita Trail (an 8.2 mile roundtrip trail starting at the Water Plant). It took us roughly 3 hours to get to Inspiration Point. But it was worth it! I love that you can still see the left over burned branches from the previous fires. Burned areas are now covered with greenary! Beautiful! A touch of the past and present all in one scene. 

Great ocean and city views! Can't get this kind of view overlooking a city and being so close to the water in San Diego.

As far as hikes near major population centers go, the inspiration point hike in Santa Barbara exceeds expectations. 

A mile or so beyond the trailhead, the civilized world--houses, roads, etc--falls away, obscured by shrub-covered and copper-colored mountain on all but one side. The main trail basically switches back and forth inside a little glen and watershed area before soaring up to the top of a ridge that affords both views of the town and ocean to the west and of a singularly majestic "plate of armor" rock formation and the mountains rising in the east.

This isn't a difficult hike, but the trail isn't clearly marked and at one point where the path splits, a right turn leads up to a utility tower. That side trail, unfortunately, is downright steep, with dry, loose soil making it a tough backtrack for beginners. Stay left to avoid this, as another reviewer recommends. 

Be prepared to park up to a mile from the trailhead on Tunnel Rd., especially if you are arriving after mid-morning.

All in all, this conveniently located, 2-4 hour hike provides an excellent way to get wrapped up in nature's embrace.

This hike is awesome. 

There are plenty of smells and places to run free.  Even some water to play in, but not a lot this time of year.  

It can be pretty hot out here so be sure to bring water.
The hike was a nice break from our awesome time on the beach.  The views down coast were incredible and we also like how close it was to Montecito where we stayed.

Before I get to the actual review of the Inspiration Point hiking trail, let me just start things off in good, inspirational spirit by tossing out a few inspirational points about hiking:

- "You're off to Great Places! Today is your day! Your mountain is waiting, so... get on your way!" -- Dr. Seuss
- "The mountains are calling and I must go." -- John Muir
- "Climbing is as close as we can come to flying." -- Margaret Young
- "Me thinks that the moment my legs begin to move, my thoughts begin to flow." -- Henry David Thoreau
- "YER A WIZARD, HARRY" -- Rubeus Hagrid

// WHO
A couple of friends and I were in Santa Barbara for the weekend, and we decided that we should take a break from eating the whole town out of business and go do some kind of outdoor activity. Naturally, before embarking on our hike to Inspiration Point, we were inspired to gorge on a huge and heavy breakfast at Jeannine's American Bakery Cafe. Wooo, go us!

// WHAT
Inspiration Point is a small summit in the Mission Canyon area of the Santa Ynez Mountains that sits 1,800 feet above Santa Barbara. It offers beautiful, breathtaking panoramic views of the city, the Pacific Ocean, and even the Channel Islands. *cue the wide-eyed 'awwws', 'whoas,' and general gasps of amazement* Inspiration Point can be accessed via either Tunnel Trail (the shorter route) or Jesusita Trail (longer and more scenic). We took the Tunnel Trail up, which actually crosses into Jesusita Trail a little before the ascent to Inspiration Point. This route is approximately a 3.8 mile out-and-back hike.

// WHEN
We began the hike at around 10:00 AM after we stuffed ourselves silly with a gluttonous breakfast. We were in a semi-comatose state and debated whether or not we really wanted to go on this hike :) Prior to the hike, we had read that it takes anywhere from 45 minutes to one hour to complete. We finished at around 11:15 AM, so not too shabby, I guess, since we almost forwent the hike to partake in some inspirational after-breakfast naps :D Woo, go us!

// WHERE
Once you pass through the metal gates at the trailhead, walk 0.75 miles to a bridge that you will need to cross over in order to leave all of civilization behind; this is the first Mission Creek crossing. Then you will travel for another mile to reach the second creek crossing. There is no bridge that goes over this part of the creek, so you will need to jump on rocks in order to cross. When we were here (early April), the creek was as dry as my sex life (except for a few muddy spots... talking about the creek, FYI), so crossing over was really easy. From here, you will ascend switchbacks for two miles until your legs turn into jello/give out or until you've reached Inspiration Point (whichever comes first), where you can join the inspiration party that will be going on. Woo, go you!

// WHY
To make ourselves feel better about being fat for uncontrollably eating nonstop throughout the entire weekend... :(

// HOW
1. Drive to the Santa Barbara Mission, but don't stop there because you will need to continue driving past it on Mission Canyon Road
2. Hit a dead end, and then turn right onto Foothill Road
3. When you see a fire station, abruptly turn left after you almost crash because you were busy ogling firefighters
4. Keep left at the fork, and continue driving until you get to the parking lot that's completely full (of course)
5. Turn around and end up parking along the side of the road (after you creepily follow people to their car so you can steal their spot)
6. Cry, complain, bitch, whine, die, etc. as you slowly make your way up the pre-hike uphill hike
7. Hike hike hike hike hike hike hike for realsies
8. Arrive at Inspiration Point and feel inspired to pass out on the ground. Instead, dramatically stare off into the horizon like a movie star and spend 20 minutes thinking deep, poetic thoughts about nothing in particular/thinking about to what eat when you get back to civilization/thinking about pooping
9. ???
10. PROFIT

// TIPS
- For those of you who love free stuff, I just want to say that this is your lucky day because parking here is free, free, FREE! No fine print included.
- Make sure your car is parked within the white lines along the side of the road and not blocking any driveways. If your car is even a little bit over, you risk getting ticketed or even TOWED... ahhh! Authorities seem pretty strict about this rule and patrol the area often, so it's probably not a good idea to test your luck.
- It's better to start your hike earlier than later due to the scarce parking situation; if you go later, you could potentially end up parking 1+ miles downhill from the trailhead, thus resulting in a pre-hike uphill hike and lots of complaining/tears.
- And last but definitely not least, bring lots of water!!! This goes for any hike though.

First time I've had the chance to run this trail since the Jesusita fire, so I thought I'd write an update. While Tunnel Trail is still closed (as of 1/9/10), Inspiration Point is open and in great condition. You can definitely see evidence of the fire all over, but the trail itself remains intact. There were some areas with loose rocks, no big deal, but I recommend wearing trail shoes if you're going to run. I didn't and it was a bit slippery, especially coming back down. Happy hiking and running!

Very cool hike.

Here is what I remember -

There is really no parking lot to speak of so you will be trying to find a spot to park on the street in a residential neighborhood. Plan on a pre-hike to get to the trail!

You take the middle road from the street up to the beginning of the trail which starts off on a paved road that winds up.

After crossing a bridge, you get to a Y and begin hiking on dirt. I'm not sure where going to the right leads. I chose the one on the left, which ended up at Inspiration Point.

On the way, you cross a creek and water pool. I suppose if there was water, there would be a small waterfall. Unfortunately there was none and no salamanders to be found. :(

The trail is deceivingly steep and rocky in spots, but overall very manageable.  And the views once you get to inspiration point are amazing.  Truly an inspiration for sure!

Round trip from the parked car was about 5 miles.

I would definitely recommend hiking shoes and wearing layers. It gets cold and damp in areas, but hot in others. Sunscreen and plenty of water is also a must.

And people....PICK UP AFTER YOUR DOG. There are bags at the entrance of the trail. USE THEM!

Amazingly beautiful hike!  I have no idea how long the hike actually is but for 2.5 hours my friends and I enjoyed this hike up to Inspiration point then back down to check out the 3 pools.  Finding the 3 pools isn't the easiest thing to do for we had to climb some rocks and hop on some rocks in the stream in order to take in the full experience, but it's worth it and great exercise!

Depending on the weather, this can be an easy to tough hike! I have no shame in sharing that when it is hot, and the sun is shining brightly, this hike can be a real pain in the booty for me.

When a friend comes for a visit and wants to go for a hike, although they haven't hiked for a year, I generally steer them to an easier one. The whole point is to enjoy your experience, right? If you are in good shape and hike often, this hike will be no problem for you. For those of you that are in decent shape and never hike, it could go either way. 

The view is truly inspirational and worth the sweat. Be sure to bring lots of water and be mindful of the bikes!

Great hike! 

I don't workout regularly, if at all, with that being said, this hike wasn't too bad. It's actually a pretty short hike if you kept a good speed. Even if you took your time, you'll be up and down in not even 4 hours - and that's a lot. Nothing too steep or strenuous. It's good beginner hike. I think there was actually some good shade in some areas. The trail is pretty easy to follow cause there's arrows on the ground. When you're on the road walking up before the dirt trail, you'll already get a taste of the view and how nice it is. Too bad we have a drought because the little streams that we cross is dried up and would've added a nice touch. Once you reach the top, the view is spectacular. Very inspiring ;) I say, if you go around 9AM, you'll be up and down by noon.
Good hike w/ the Mrs. 1.5 hrs. Nice view of SB. Wish it was longer
Bomb hike! The trail is awesome. The view was ok, but it's worth it. It's 3.6 miles round trip. I believe.

If Mission Canyon were a chapter book, then the hike to Inspiration Point would be the poetically written introduction. This hike provides a nice glimpse into the fascinating geology and beauty of the area. In a short 3.5 miles and about 1800 feet, you'll cross a creek, hike through shadier areas dense with wild flowers and beautiful foliage, and see more canyon-like topography near the top. The entire hike is gentle and extraordinarily scenic. You've reached Inspiration Point when you come to a rocky ledge where you can admire the sparkling Pacific Ocean and Channel Islands beyond. Some people like to picnic up here, but PLEASE bring everything out with you that you brought in (of course)!

The trail is wide enough to accommodate two in most areas and is well kept, making it easy to hike or run. Since the distance and terrain is so mild, I recommend it for families and pets. Observe the areas you like and then plan deeper explorations of Mission Canyon. If you like the sun exposed, canyon terrain, then check out Tunnel Trail next, for a longer, more challenging vertical hike. If you require something more physically demanding you'll like the hike to Cathedral Peak which involves a lot of bouldering and class III rock climbing. From the top of Inspiration Point, turn around and admire the hillsides. The rocky, imposing looking one in front of you is Cathedral Peak. See all of those rocks? Good, you'll be climbing those!

Inspiration Point is best enjoyed just before sunset. Hike up and run down. It's heaven!

For 3.5 miles this should take less than 45 minutes (depending on your speed). So it's a nice one for impatient hikers who seek a scenic vista without much effort or time.
This is a short hike that will give you a good workout. The only bad part of it is the parking. It has an amazing view as well.

I headed here around 11am on a Thursday.  There's plenty of parking along Tunnel Rd although you may have to walk a ways.  The path itself wasn't too busy.  However there was a lot of dog crap along the beginning of the trail, which is a shame considering the bags to scoop are provided at the trail head.  I recommend taking a phone with GPS and following along to ensure you're choosing the correct path.  There are a few options, none of which are too difficult for someone that's fit and able.

The view is awesome, just be sure to know where you're going before you leave for the point. 

Enter this in Google Maps to get there:
Spyglass Ridge Rd, Santa Barbara, CA 93105
------------------------------------------------
When you're on Mission Canyon Road, you'll have to park on the side street. There's no public parking, and be sure to be within the curb to not get towed. 

Once you're up there, go right of the water tower and KEEP HEADING on the FIRST LEFT of every fork in the trail until you're at the top of the mountain. From there, cross the junction and you'll see a wonderful view of Santa Barbara. 

The hike is easy but not totally flat, so bring 2 bottles of water per person since the hike itself is about 2 hours round trip and on a summer afternoon, it can get pretty hot.

Awesome 7 mile hike in Santa Barbara. This hike is a ZOO here! I've never seen this many animals on one hike. There were flying squirrels, ducks, lizards everywhere, and a snake.

The trail is well maintained. Be sure to follow signs at the crossroads. You will reach the peak with an elevation of 1300 feet that overlooks the beautiful Santa Barbara, the land of great pinot noir. There's a steady uphill climb, but nothing too steep.
Beautiful hike! Just watch out for poison oak along the trail. It's easy to brush up against in a few different places

Map to the Mission church at 2201 Laguna Street. From there, head up Los Olivos toward the mountains. The road makes a jag and becomes Mission Canyon Rd.

At the stop sign, turn right on Foothill Rd.

At the stop sign and fire station, turn left onto Mission Canyon Rd.

At the fork in the road, stay left on Tunnel Rd.

Drive to the end of Tunnel Rd. and park your car. Be careful to park with your tires completely within the line or you will be towed. They are not kidding about that.

Hike up to the end of the road by the large water tank and metal gate across the road. This is the trailhead (go between the iron gates). 

To stay on the right trail, basically keep forking left, and choose signs for Jesusita Trail. There will be a part where you dip down to cross a (now dry) creek / riverbed,but keep going and you'll hit the mile of uphill switchbacks. That takes you to the view you wanted to see!

Pretty nice hike.  My (8 month pregnant) wife and I did this hike on a nice sunny spring day.  My GPS read almost exactly five miles from where we parked round-trip on this hike.  The view was pretty awesome, but I haven't to say that the view from the start of the hike is pretty incredible as well.

It's a pretty busy trail, and we saw a number of people running the trail.  However, I think if you really want to run the trail it's probably best to get out there earlier otherwise you will be dodging people the whole run.  

The signage isn't great, but when we went there were plenty of people around and it wasn't a problem figuring out where to go.  There were a lot of little side trails and if I lived nearby I'd definitely try checking out some of the other trails.

Perfect!! Great hike for all levels! A little muddy after the rain but tons of fun and beautiful setting. Perfect for kids and dogs - didn't make it to the top due to timing but plan to go back next time we are in SB!!

Relatively easy to find (end of Tunnel road next to the water tower) and not too hard to climb. At least half is actually paved, the top portion is fairly steep and over loose gravel. We went on a week day so we found curb side parking pretty close the the trail head and easily got all the way inside the lines. We also didn't encounter too many people but enough to know we were going the right direction. There are trails here and there that veer off that aren't clearly labeled, so when in doubt aim for the power line towers. The view/visibility is going to be largely dependent on what the weather is doing and the air quality.

Go hike inspiration point NOW! Spring time is here, it just rained, and the colors are vivid and the flowers are blooming! I felt like I was on a mushroom trip the whole time! The hike also has adventure to it! We had to jump rocks over a rapid creek. The view at the top is magnificent. Make sure you have a map or guidebook

I almost got killed by a treacherous baby snake 2 minutes into the hike, but s'all good (sense the sarcasm?) Thankfully my best friend saved me from such a fatality! I loved this hike because its not too strenuous and lengthy compared to 7 Falls. You still get the view of gorgeous Santa Barbara without the extra time and work. If you don't want to go on intense hike like 7 Falls then give Inspiration Point a try!

Did the 7 mile loop on a whim to test my new hiking shoes that I just bought. The hike was easy and very picturesque. It's a walk in the park;)

Some rocky areas but was pretty much easy. It was a little over an hour or so hike.

Still a class act. I just ran the trail in its entirety for the first time since the winter--still sparkling and in excellent condition. It seems that after the winter rains, there were a few reinforcements placed on the steeper parts of the trail toward the summit. But things are so solid out there, you could practically do this in sandals. I ran it in my Nike Frees--a tractionless shoe which simulates barefoot running--and there was absolutely no dirt or rock slippage. It was a nice, smooth ride. 

Of course, the summit at sunset on a late summer afternoon is one of those simple pleasures in life that should absolutely not be missed. I try not to, at least.

The reviews here are confusing and misleading; Inspiration Point hike is only 3.5 miles ROUND TRIP!

The greatest elevation is about 800 feet, lending a view of Santa Barbara and the ocean at the top. There's some tower with wires up there too.

This is an easy hike because of its brevity and the fact most of it is a steady paved road. I consider it more of a walk than actual hiking and can do the whole thing in under one hour. The toughest part is a fairly steep incline to get to the tower, but it's brief. Last time I went a woman was running up and down this incline multiple times and she wasn't a pro athlete.

If you're looking for an easy hike or something you can do quickly after work, this is a good hike. If you're in fairly good condition and want a challenge, this is only a warm up.

Beautiful hike! I wish the creek had water but anyways it was nice for running up and trail running down! If you're into trail running it's pretty exciting jumping over boulders and rocks on the trail!
Once at the top you can go down from the main peak to a more private area. It's nicer and not a lot of people come down for some reason. 

Beautiful panorama view! Highly recommend.

Great hike but it's not easy. 2400 elevation gain and 3.8 miles from trail head. You have another 1- 2 miles on the paved road to the trail head. View is beautiful... Bring lots of water... It's hot and not much shade. Would be beautiful at sunset.

Great view of the city and ocean from inspiration point. There's only street parking here, and it was difficult to find due to other hikers getting out earlier (Saturday ~11 AM). The hike was a little steep in a few places, but it was relatively short. With a break at the top it was about 2 hours total.
Took this hike this morning. 3 out of 5 for difficulty. Great hike up. About an hour. Views up at the top are well worth it. Not a lot of parking so go early.

3.5 Stars

We used the intersection of Tunnel Rd and Mission Rd for the GPS.  As the road forks, we stayed toward Tunnel Rd and parked on the right side.  A regular told us that they love to tow here, and if you're sticking out just a little or if your wheels aren't turned...byebye to your car.  He also told us that even if you make it out while they are hooking up your car and they haven't exactly towed it away yet...it costs $450 to get it unhooked.  OMG.  We parked as close as we could to the little hillside without falling off and completely turned our wheels.

After you carefully park, head toward the iron gate/go through the iron gate and you'll be on your merry way.  Follow signs for Jerusita Trail when the road forks and shortly after, you'll be at the top.

This route is pretty easy, I would say level 4 out of 10.  I may have been overly mentally prepared as I didn't do thorough research and I was expecting a 7 mile hike.  Taking the Tunnel Rd route felt in the neighborhood of 4.25 miles   It took my husband and I (and our 7 year old chiweenie) about 1 hour and 45 minutes round trip.  

Pros:
* Dog Friendly!
* Quick turn around time
* Friendly peeps

Cons:
* Bugs!  Bring Bug Spray.
* Little to no shade
* the actual Inspiration Point is tiny, you wouldn't be able to stand around and enjoy for long if there are lots of hikers.
