Signs are very difficult to understand.  Go in the morning so there's plenty of people to help direct you.  I meant to get to 7 falls but the bottom was dry (no water on bottom) and someone said it may be better with a hiking buddy to help boost you up rocks.   They said there was water up too.  

Got to the top and it was a decent view.  Not easy but not hard.  I usually like a burn and I got some.  Rolling hills in the beginning.  Paved in the beginning and then trail higher.  Google maps has good trail lines on it so use that to help guide you.  Try Alltrails or Mapmyhike app too.

Great short-ish hike for families.  Starts very easy (paved trail, moderate grade) and then turns into a steep, rocky single-track trail to the look-out.  Could be difficult for very small children.  Bring a water bottle or 2, its very exposed and hot in parts.  The view of the coast and the Santa Cruz islands is worth it.  Took us 1h 15min with kids 10 and 12, and we clocked it at 3.5 miles out and back from the water tower/gate.
Trail in much need of maintenance.  Rains have taken their toll.  MTF apparently no longer permitted to perform maintenance by SB Co.  Great hike when trail healthy and the views are spectacular.

I had one and a half hours to kill and was determined to explore the Santa Barbara hills. At 3.6 miles round-trip with a moderate climb, Inspiration Point via the Tunnel Trail was pretty much the perfect choice. 

Early spring is probably the best time of the year to hike the Santa Barbara front-country. Bright green vegetation, wildflowers, lively streams, and 70 degree sunny weather...

I'll take this hike over State Street shopping any day.

An hour-and-a-half hike with beautiful views up top.  Probably a level 7/10 since the whole way up is uphill.  Make sure to go on a cool day otherwise a hot day would make the uphill climb very difficult!

Great view! We hiked Jesusita trail up to Inspiration Point, it was 7.44 miles round trip. About 10 minutes before we made it up, I was ready to quit, but I am so glad I didn't. It's worth it. Go early, there is parking and it isn't as hot. There isn't much shade once you gain elevation. 

Seriously, go hike it. You will be glad you did.

Awesome hike just minutes from downtown.  Chose this over the botanical gardens and I think we made the right decision.  According my iphone's GPS it was exactly 3.5 miles, and took my wife and me with the baby in a backpack about 1 hr 45 mins back to the car.  That included almost no time at the top but a few stops to tend to the kiddo.  The vertical climb is about 800' so it will definitely make you breathe heavy but it's not extreme.  The trail is about 40% paved, 40% smooth trail and 20% medium difficulty.  No scrambling or technical areas, though the one creek crossing could be dicey for the less sure-footed I suppose.  Or if the creek flow was higher.  We got lucky with a parking spot right near the gate, but at 1 pm on a tuesday there were a LOT of cars parked.  You might want to be patient if there's not a spot immediately and wait for someone to return and leave vice parking way down the hill.  The views along the way are nice, with the water (and oil platforms...) in partial view.  The view from the top is gorgeous!  You can see all along the coast, the islands and just about the whole city.  Best of all, it's free!

Staying in an Airbnb in Santa Barbara, our hosts suggested that we take a hike up to Inspiration Point. It was definitely worth it! 

It took my boyfriend and I about 2 hours each way and even though we're active people, there were definitely some steep challenges. What I can say is that the view at Inspiration Point is well worth the hike!! It's an amazing panoramic view of Santa Barbara from downtown, to the ocean, mountains, and the horizon.

This is an easy hike that's good to take visitors, or folks who don't hike regularly... but it's not all that inspirational. If you're going in the Summer, start early, because this trail is dry and hot.

Perfect level of difficulty for someone with not so much experience hiking. I believe it's 3.8 miles to the top, and it's totally worth it. The top has a flat area with rocks that you can sit on and enjoy the view of Santa Barbara. We came across several fellow hikers, all of which were very friendly. Overall, it was a fun hike and I would probably do it again if I ever find myself in Santa Barbara.
I thought it was a nice hike and really steep towards the end. Took us under 2 hours there and back.

Walking along the near the bottom of the hike, my friends and I asked a couple walking down the trail, "is this the trail to Inspiration Point?" and they reply "I don't know, we never made it there." 

*friends and I give each other uneasy looks*

Well we made it there! :D

The trail is actually easy to follow. A great workout in the beginning because it is a paved road and would have been great for people to go jogging on, on a regular basis. The trail then switches to a dirt trail, well maintained and the farther in you go it gets shadier. Some rocky points in the trail, and you will rock hop across a creek. 

I went during early March, so there is tons of greenery and wild flowers! So pretty and picturesque. Once you get to the top, you'll know it and you'll have a lovely panoramic view of downtown Santa Barbara and the ocean.

Trail takes a little over an hour at a leisurely pace one way. 3.5 miles round trip. There were a lot of people, but it didn't feel crowded. Parking is in the neighborhood at the bottom of the trail. Street parking and rather limited. If you come late, the farther down the hill you park. Walking uphill to get to the trail head from your parking space is quite a workout in itself!

This hike was a nice workout & not too busy. Very rocky at points, and little shade so definitely go early or in the evening. At the top there were great views of Santa Barbara & the ocean. We weren't able to see the islands though due to fog. Stay to the left at Y's & you'll be good.

Positive: A really great hike with a beautiful view at the top.

Negative: Nearly impossible to find parking and locate the gates of the trail.

Without really looking at directions or guide, our aim was actually the Seven Falls/3 Pools hike; however, we made the wrong turn.

We came in the afternoon (around 2) on a Saturday at the end of Sept. Santa Barbara was warm but not overbearing. We luckily found spots as people were leaving but it was still crowded. 

We were in a group and I guess as they say, try to stay in a group. My friend and I weren't the ones to look  the hike,  yet we were the ones trailblazing for our group (and way ahead of them too). A lady going up said always stay to the left to get there. 

Well we got to what was supposed to be the stream (it was dry and no water) and didn't pay much attention to it . 

NOTE: THIS IS WHERE THE TRAILS BRANCH OVER. If you go upstream, you'll get to Seven Falls. If you cross the stream, you'll head over to Inspiration Point. 

Well we kept going up wondering how there would be a fall at this height. We got up to the top (total of about 45 min of walking) and saw all of Santa Barbara, the ocean, and surrounding landscape. It was a great view.

If you have the choice of the Seven Falls and Inspiration Point, don't go to Seven Falls if it's in the dry season or not raining. There will be stagnant water that's pretty much nasty. 

If you want a fun, challenging walk/downhill, go downstream from the fork. We went during the dry season so there wasn't any water. We thought we were going the wrong way but it connects to the bridge and is a shortcut (though much harder). There isn't much of a trail so you will have to follow what should be the waterlines. Be prepared to jump onto rocks the whole time.

Total distance going up to Inspiration Point and the downstream was about 2.55 mi.

Overall, it was a good hike. Not super challenging but you can take it at your own pace. There's a great view that is worth seeing.
Don't go up the rocky road to the right unless you're ready for a challenge. At the fork, you want to choose the small dirt road to the left.

This is an intermediate level hike, some uphill and loose rocks but generally doable if you are of average fitness. 

There are long paths of unshaded areas so wear sunscreen. If you are driving park along the side of the road. There were plenty of people and dogs on the hike so you will have to share. 

I got confused when I got to the top but keep going straight and you will get to the promised land.

Everyone seems to be mainly interested in the panoramic city/ocean view of Santa Barbara, Goleta and the Channel Islands that is available at Inspiration Point. But what i always most love about this hike are the BEAUTIFUL ROCK-OUTCROP FORMATIONS. The almost perfectly-triangular Arlington Peak is one GORGEOUS sandstone peak and all the formations to the left, right and foreground offer amazing patterns of striated boulders (many of them vertical in their alignment). I have almost no knowledge of geology, but these sandstone formations with their subtle hues of beige, gold, coral pink, etc.,  are really exquisite, not readily seen elsewhere in most hiking country around the USA. When i first moved to Santa Barbara in the late 1980s, i fell in love with these sandstone boulders, which dot the trails of most of S.Barbara's coastal mountain hikes. 

The roughly 3.4 mile Jesusita Trail hike from the water filtration plant (you can also access it lower down from Stevens Park) or the shorter (roughly 2.5 miles) Tunnel Road hike, both lead up to Inspiration Point, and will richly reward anyone who loves not only the ocean & city views but also these marvelous geologic treasures.

And yes, if/when we ever get some decent rainfall again (a big El Nino weather system is promised for Fall-Winter 2015-16), the creeks and pools are very nice to enjoy, too.
This a great hike - not too challenging but enough elevation on a hot day to get the sweat glands operating. Take a dog if you can get one. The view of city & bay is spectacular from the top.

Great hike! Be sure to wear sunscreen, as this trail is not overly shaded.  

First and foremost know that there is no parking area designated to hikers, so you'll need to find parking alongside of the road. Just be sure you're pulled over enough and not IN the road (you will be towed). Also read the signs; this is a residential area and some areas are not for parking. We had about a .5 mile walk just to get from our car to the trailhead, but no biggie. 

Once you get to the gate, you'll have a decent amount of paved area to walk before hitting a dirt trail. To make sure you stay on the right path, keep to your left and follow the signs for the Jesusita Trail. There was a lot of people on the trail (Saturday morning), so getting lost wasn't really a concern for us anyways. 

The trail has its steep parts, but definitely manageable. I would say due to the terrain (rocky and steep in areas) it is a moderate hike. It's not too long of a hike though! Depending on where you park, you'll get in 3-4 miles. This would be great for beginners. We took our time, relaxed at the top and were in and out in about 1.5 hours. When you get to the top and see the view, it will be entirely worth it! 

The view was beautiful and the hike was a perfect challenge. We had a great time! :) I would definitely recommend this trail.

This is a real hike!

Dogs are allowed! HELL YA!
Uphill and half paved/half trail. 
The view is truly inspiring. ;]
Wilderness and no worries about being in someone's backyard or trespassing.

No water which just proves California is in a serious drought, but it was still pretty none the less. 

Parking is street parking and not very much of it. I got lucky, but I would prepare for a bit of a walk from your car to the trail head if you don't find a good spot.

Poo bags are available at the beginning of the trail head so please pack out your dog's poop!

There is one trail and then it forks off at the trail sign and you can either go left to the water and then up a hill again or right and straight up another hill to a tower. I chose right and my hike was short, but steep. I'll be back to check out the left trail.

:( Meant to go to 7 Falls for a little climbing adventure but ended up going to inspiration point since we missed the point at which we were suppose to turn since the creek was almost dried up (we were suppose to go right, but went left). Funny thing, I had done this hike 6 years ago and was trying to avoid this hike because I remember it being all up-hill and not that pretty...but fate took us up here. Dang you fate!!! AHHH!!! 

Ok, maybe I'm being dramatic but I am not a fan of hikes that are all up-hill.  I much rather have some variety in the inclines and not be out of breath the whole way. Also, there isn't that much nature to be seen along this trail (it's not very forest-y) and no nice creeks to look at.  It doesn't help that my bf and I were both wearing pants (We wore pants in anticipation of climbing rocks at 7 falls) and it was a hot day.  I ended up talking my shirt off because it was just too warm. We had to take several breathers because I spend way more time eating and yelping than I do exercising these days. -_- The incline really got to me.  The top is aite. A good view of SB/Goleta if there isn't too much smog.  The hike down is very fast because you're almost jogging because you can't help it.  This is where you realize that you should wear shoes with good ankle support because you can twist your ankle on the rocks/uneven surfaces.  Luckily, by the time we got down, we were happy and forgot about the pain of climbing, climbing, climbing up-hill. 

The parking situation is pretty lame too.  You just park along side the road (within the white lines so you don't get a ticket) and then hike up the residential hill til you get to the the water tower-and then you keep walking up the asphalt for what seems like a long time until you reach the actual trail head. 

Tips:
Don't wear pants
Bring lots of water
Be in decent shape before doing this 
Wear shoes with good ankle support 
Make sure you know where you're going so you don't go on the wrong trail 
Come early to avoid the crowd and get better parking

Of the two ways to get there, we entered Mission Canyon along Spyglass Ridge Road and Jesusita Trail to reach Inspiration Point. Without spaces at the trailhead, parking is in the residential area along Tunnel Road. Starting at the portable toilet on Tunnel Road with perpendicular parking, the hike was 4.0 miles round trip with a 1000' elevation gain.

Aside from Inspiration Point, the hike was rather lousy because half of it was paved. Follow the forks to the left when going to Inspiration Point because the trail also splits off to Arlington and Cathedral Peaks and Seven Falls.

An uncreative name for a hike, this was the fifth different Inspiration Point I've trekked. It's like naming a lake as blue or calling a town Greenville. It's also not really a "point" but the top of a hill. I must admit despite the sucky parking and the sucky paved road, the view was quite nice with a panoramic vista of Santa Barbara and Goleta, obviously the Pacific Ocean, and the backed by the Channel Islands.

Nice, easy hike. Great for kids and dogs....but so many people are on this trail. It was noisy and not peaceful at all. I was stuck behind the chattiest hikers of all time! So if you want a nice fast workout, this is great. If you are looking for zen, take the route on the right, tunnel trail.

This is a great, easy hike while you're in Santa Barbara.  Parking at the trailhead is limited, so there's a good chance you'll have to find parking farther down along the side of the road.  Dog friendly, some pools for your pooch to play in.  As always, bring plenty of water for yourself and your four-legged friend.  Not much tree cover, so I can see where this would be a sweaty climb on a hot day.
More photos:  http://www.roamingtogether.com/photos/

Jim & Colette
http://www.roamingtogether.com/

Awesome hike we went to the right side, super super steep goes to a lil look out, came down and then went on the left trail up to the real look out absolutely beautiful!!! Hike was good up hill most it my phones said 6miles.
Very nice hike with a beautiful view at the top! Not an avid hiker and I thought it was very managable!

If you like hikes that are not strenuous on a sunny day then this hike is for you. I went with a group of six people and it was quite an exercise with plenty ponds, streams and vegetation for observation and enjoyment.  The hike can be done in about 1.5 hours with occasional stops and time for viewing Santa Barbara at the top of the hill.  There were occasional inclines that left me breathless once at the top, but I was trying to keep pace with the other half of my group minutes ahead, but had I walked leisurely I would have been not as tired.  I would definitely suggest this hike to families with kids. It is a refreshing hike with multiple paths to take.

Inspiration Point trail is a easy to moderate hike. 

The trail can be reached from the Tunnel Road trail (I've done it and this review is based off that) or from the Jesusita Trail (which I have not done). 

About the hike- Approximately 4.5 miles roundtrip, the trail does not loop. Perfect for morning or late afternoon, although there are few shaded areas you don't want to be hiking in the strong midday sun. The trail is not just uphill but varies between level, uphill, and downhill throughout all while making an elevation gain of about 800ft.

How to get there- Take Foothills Road Mission Canyon Road, when the road forks keep left onto Tunnel Road which you will take until the end and park. Walk along the paved road until you reach the trail head.

At the top- Once you reach the top the view is, well, inspirational. Spectacular views of Santa Barbara and the ocean. Great picture opportunity.

There are reptiles, birds, and other wildlife on the hike. 
It's a great location with other trails in the area to try as well.

I would say this is a fairly moderate hike. The way up is mostly uphill but doable. We went on a Tuesday morning and saw a decent amount of people and their dogs there. The first mile on the trail is paved and then it turns into dirt. It is important to stay to the left at the "Tunnel Trail" sign and the yellow rusted sign that doesn't really say anything. Once we got to the top it was worth it. Such a pretty view of Santa Barbara and the ocean! The way down was all downhill so the round trip was about three hours. Would definitely recommend for anyone looking for a moderate hike with a view!

I had wanted a hike in Santa Barbara that would work up a bit of a sweat, had awesome views and wasn't too long, being that I was only in town for a weekend and had a lot of other activities on the agenda. Thanks to my fellow Yelpers, I found that Inspiration Point fit the bill. 
As others have mentioned, parking is very limited and to get to the trailhead you essentially end up on a dead end street. As you make your way to the trail, and start to see a lot of cars parked along side the road, grab the first spot you see, making sure your tires are within the white lines and not blocking a driveway!

Now to the hike...On a warm day, even in the early morning, it gets hot pretty quickly. We got to the trail around 9:30 and it already was quite toasty, even in late November. The first portion of the trail is paved and winds it way up towards the junction of Tunnel Trail and the Jesuita Trail. At that point, the pavement ends and you're on rougher terrain. To make it to the Inspiration Point summit, bear left (I think it's the Jesuita section) and that heads up to to the summit. At one point, you will go straight over a creek, which was dry this time of year and you pick up the trail directly on the other side. Nearing the summit, you do have some minor rocks and loose gravel to contend with, but overall, I considered this a "moderate" trail. 

It was fairly well travelled and we ran in to a lot of friendly hikers. In that sense it felt safe enough to possibly hike alone, albeit, telling others of your plans. Along the way we were rewarded with a variety of bird sightings, lizards and of course views galore. Once you hit the top, take a breath and then take in the magical views of Santa Barbara, the ocean and additional mountain views. Lots of photo ops, naturally and it was a crystal clear day.

Wear sturdy shoes. Even though it's not a long hike (less than 4 miles I'd say-we ended up just doubling back the way we came up), you still have some slippery terrain, smaller boulders to scramble around and some loose gravel on the trail. Lovely scenery but be mindful of keeping to the trail and others coming up towards you or behind you-there were several runners that we had to move for, which was fine-just make sure to pay attention.

A great, shorter hike to start your day off right. We felt the burn from going up-which is not a straight up, but a gradual series of switchbacks. It was enough to work up a decent sweat but definitely not something you are completely out of breath for.

Overall, highly recommended. Hooray Nature!.

We throughly enjoyed ourselves hiking inspiration point. This is a great hike for beginners like myself. We did the hike in about an hour and half round trip. I read some other reviews that the trail was not well maintained, but I did not feel this was the case. 

Views from the tail were absolutely stunning! If we lived near by I could see this being a regular hike for us. 

It was only around 68 degrees and it felt very warm. There is not much shade along the trail so come prepared for a lot of sun. Only other downside was the minuscule amount of parking, I would imagine weekends fill up very quickly.
Moderate hike with rocky terrain in parts. Approximately 4 miles to top and back. Beautiful panoramic view from the top.

There seems to be at least 4 different trails that lead to Inspiration Point, one being Jesusita Trail which I took to get here, Stevens Park is another, & the other 2 I forgot but once you make it up to the peak you can see there are two other paths that lead here.

This spot has some great views of to the south & west of Santa Barbara County. Despite the ocean being overcast I still got some decent shots of the view, but trust me the picture don't do this spot that much justice. Even though this is just my first hike of a mountain in the Santa Barbara area getting up to this point for the view is totally worth it.

Inspiration Point is a beautiful hike that is very popular with locals. For that reason, we recommend starting this hike as early as possible in order to score a parking spot. Otherwise, be prepared to make a long and very steep climb before you even reach the trailhead. We managed to find a spot about a half mile away. So yeah, we started off with a schlep before even hitting the trail.

This is a heart-pounding 3.5 mile, out and back trail. And as you guessed, it takes you up a mountain to get some incredible views. Who knows, maybe you'll be inspired? We can tell you that we were at least out of breath. Though, that doesn't have quit the same ring as "Inspiration Point," right?

It is a pretty straight forward trail. Though, we do recommend following along on the All Trails app. The main reason? There is a very tricky turn off, a sharp left, that is about halfway through the hike. More than likely, you'll find other hikers on this trail so just follow them if you don't have the map. 

The All Trails App, particularly the real-time map tracker, helps keep our motivation up. After all, we love to know when we're almost done. That's our inspiration -- paired with a post-hike brunch.

Distance to top:  about 2.5 miles
Good for runners: Yes
Good for bikers: No
Good for kids: Nope (too many hills & inclines)
Loops: No
Shade: Not much shade.

Difficulty: easy to moderate.  But it's all up-hill
Parking: You gotta park in the neighborhood down the hill, and parking is really limited.  If you arrive late, you gotta walk a long distance to get to the gate.

What really annoys me about this trail is the first 3/4 - 1 mile is just walking on a concrete road.  I brought hiking shoes so my feet were killing me walking on asphalt.  Had I known that I would've brought running shoes too.  As such, it's not really a hike until you are 1/3 of the way in.  

I think this trail is great for runners but bad for hikers.

We hiked here late afternoon and got one of the last parking spaces before the road ends at the water tower. You'll hike up a fire road for a while before hitting the trail head. We talked to a few folks who were headed to or coming from the right branch, so we headed left up to inspiration point. The observation point is not very far from the trail head but it's a steep climb. Wonderful tranquility and views all along the trail and at the overlook (at the power line tower). We went in September and the sun was still pretty far north. Had hoped to see it set over Santa Barbara. Would like to come back closer to the solstice to see how much further south the sun sets. Nice view of Santa Barbara. In my photo the mission is in the middle and the harbor to the left.

UPDATE: after reading a description of this hike on AllTrails, I'm not sure we were on the right trail. Anyway, we had a steep, but nice hike up to the power lines, and were rewarded with a nice view.

Not what I expected.  It gets pretty steep near the top with loose rocks for the last 1/4 mile.  There's power lines behind you at the top.  The panoramic views are heavily dependent on the weather (fog was present along the coast all day, so I was able to see the ocean).  There's no shade going uphill and it turns from paved to dirt road about half way up.
Very difficult the last stretch where it's a 60 degree incline. Otherwise beautiful but short hike

My favorite hike of our trip was as inspiring as we had hoped. It took us a little over an hour to get to the top, and less than an hour coming back down; this included a few brief stops in the shade to catch my breath, and leisurely taking photos at the point. With hardly any signage, we trusted our GPS and the "keep bearing left" tip. A bottle of water per person was just right, and we were glad for the hat/SPF/sunglasses recommendations from other reviews. We got lucky with a parking spot close to the trailhead; I can see how coming during peak hours could add a 10-15 minute walk to your vehicle.

I wish I'd brought a headlamp and timed it for sunset, but it was a breathtaking sight just before noon nonetheless, and seeing the change in lighting on the intermediate greenery was lovely. Lots of local dogs being run along the trail, most on-leash. The only wildlife we spotted were some small lizards, and a murder of crows overhead. The scrambly bits start shortly after crossing the creek, which is a few minutes past the end of the paved section of the path. Grippy shoes are your friend, and there are only a few spots where traction gets sketchy. Only two annoying bits of graffiti at the top; multiple vantage points at the plateau and just beyond to the left. We'll definitely come back to do this one again.

There are 2 hikes I recommend most to anyone who asks me for recommendations in Santa Barbara County and the first one is Inspiration Point (also known as Inspiration Peak)!

This trail has an ample amount of parking at the trail head, is not hard to find with good directions, takes the perfect amount of time to where anyone can do it in a normal work day before or after (about an hour roundtrip to two hours depending on your pace), is well laid out so you're not going straight up or downhill for too long, and has the perfect amount of scenery as you stroll along.

Obviously the best part is the view at the peak, but also right as you come up to the last strut on the way to the peak there is a rock to your right that is called lover's kiss as it looks like two people are passionately getting down in rock formation. I will post a pic up next time I go but be on the lookout and maybe you'll see it without my help!

Awesome trail for any and all ages, number one for families and groups in the area, not very time consuming, and a beautifully inspiring view (pictures don't do justice) where you can see a vast amount of Santa Barbara from the tippy top. If you haven't been, just try it, you'll thank me!

One word: INSPIRATIONAL :D

It's a great hike which took my two friends and I going up about 45 minutes with some upward incline, lizard spotting, greeting fellow hikers and watching not to trip and fall lol

At the top you can see all of Santa Barbara, Goleta, and UCSB. Pretty cool view with sunny weather to accommodate it :)

I hated this hike :( I really wanted to love it and I THOUGHT it would be a great choice going by all the reviews but it was so packed that it wasn't even fun. We went mid-morning on a Tuesday and the neighborhood was super congested with cars before we even got to the trail. There were sooo many people, like Runyon Canyon on a weekend status. Groups of girls talking loudly, families with crying kids in tow, just not my cup of tea. If you don't care about being surrounded by a million other people while out in nature try it out. I personally prefer more quiet and secluded trails.

As of 6/29, this hike is open to the public. We started at the Inspiration Point Trailhead and it's about a 4mile, 2-3 hour hike of moderate intensity. The first half is paved and the second half requires going over some rocks so recommend a good pair of hiking shoes. Like others have said, the trail isn't well marked, but if you keep making lefts at each fork, you'll get to the destination. On a clear day, you'll get great views of Santa Barbara and also be able to see the Channel Islands. 
Minus-1 Star for the parking situation. There is NO parking lot and very minimal street parking so be prepared to take a "mini-hike" from wherever you find parking to the start of the trailhead.

This hike this morning was awesome, it came highly recommended by the workers at the hostel I'm staying at, so I went to give it a try.

Upon driving up to the area, be aware that you need to park on the side of the road, so if there are a lot of cars up ahead, just grab the first spot you see and get ready for the uphill climb to the base of the trails
.
So then you reach the trailhead, and already, you have views. Beautiful scenic shots of the ocean and SB, beautiful.

The first part of the trail is paved and comfortable. Easy walk up the path and a fun little waterfall along the way, it was nice.

Then, once the path becomes dirt and unpaved, the real hiking begins. And I went towards Inspiration Point up the hills, and I'd say it's a low Medium-Easy hike to get up there. But remember, bring water. There are no fountains.

Also, be careful of THE MUD.  The mud was pretty thick on the way up, and it almost ate my shoe as I was trudging through it. So be wary. And also, enjoy the dogs on the path.

The waterfalls are beautiful, and the scene from the top is breathtaking. This hike is a 5 star hike.

Apparently there's an easier trail up to Inspiration Point than I took.  This review is for access via Jesusita Trail.  The trail itself is one of the more picturesque trails I've been on.  It meanders it's way four miles up the mountain side through stream crossings, tree canopy shaded canyons, over rocks and by big boulders.  If you plan on mountain biking this trail, plan on pushing your bike a great deal of the way up, as the steepness of the trail in many areas makes it difficult to gain enough momentum to clear the rocks.  I consider myself an advanced intermediate rider, but had to get off and on so many times that I finally gave up and just pushed.  After about mile two, the trail does get a little easier and you can begin to see the city and ocean down below.  Once to Inspiration Point, you'll have a breathtaking 180 degree view, (if you have any breath left after the climb).  The ride down is, of course  much easier, but still fairly technical, so bring all of your MTB skills or leave the bike at home.  I would recommend this as a hike more than a moutain bike ride, but maybe that's the weakling in me talking.

The views at the top are so worth the trek. 

Hiked the trail to Inspiration Point this morning from the end of Tunnel Road. Some mention that it's a short hike - and they're correct, but it's still a good 2+ hours of activity. 

The hike is mostly uphill to the point and can be fairly deceiving as there aren't really specific markers. I'd read other reviews where they state to take a left at the "yield" sign. The area mentioned did not have a yield sign (it appears to have been removed), but I did note an upside-down brown triangle which I believe was the bottom portion of the original sign. You will want to take a left here. It's the rockier/narrower of the two paths at this particular fork but it's the correct one. 

The trail was fairly busy when I hiked it this morning. I must say that it was in very good condition. Whomever maintains it has been doing a great job. The majority of it meanders through beautiful local vegetation and is just gorgeous. 

The prize is at the top where you get to view Santa Barbara panoramically. What a view. There are plenty of spots to stand and take in the views and sounds of the nearby birds (and chickens in a coop down on Tunnel Road along the street parking area - you can hear them clucking away even from the top!). 

Not an easy hike but a very rewarding one to say the least. I will be back soon.

A beautiful hike in Santa Barbara, that gets you away from the crowd. Gorgeous, just tough enough to feel proud you worked hard, and not too long. There are some areas where you are climbing rocks as stairs, and it can be strenuous. We typically get up and down in just under two hours, moving at a steady pace and depending on how far down the hill we had to park.

There are lots of dogs on and off leash, but so far I haven't had any issues with aggressive dogs. Parking was a bit tough, as there is no designated spot. We have been twice now, and simply parked on the side of the road. If you go after 7am there will be plenty of people there already parked so you can simply follow the crowds.

A beautiful hike!

One of my favorite trails that I can't rate a 5 star is because of parking. I'm like the parking reviewer of SB, nothing worse to me than arriving and having to feel like leaving because parking is horrendous or there are just no options. On a weekend, this can be the case at Inspiration point because it parking is on street, on a windy road, on a hill, in a neighborhood. So, go early and on weekdays. Once on the trail, it's heaven. Yep, that's 5 star status. Fun, beautiful, a goal to get to the top and back, no complaints. Just remember, bring water and a happy heart to smile at bad drivers that can't park, like me!

This is an extremely easy and fun hike, doable by pretty much anyone between 5 and 65 years old.  30 minutes or less to the top if you are going for time, about 45 minutes at an easy pace, and 1-1.5 hours if you are at an extremely leisurely pace or with children who stop to look at lizards and such. 

Parking is an issue if you arrive later, which is why I prefer to be in by 0600 when the extra hike along the road is less than 1/4 mile. Average is about 8 spots on the side of the narrow road, every 1/4 mile, so if you show up with the late crowd after 0800 be prepared. Traffic caution is recommended, especially with children and animals. (Dogs welcome, no water available on the trail)

Start on the service road for the first half, then break off to the left when the trail gives you the opportunity. There is also a trail off to the right just before the left side trail, which is a bit steeper and longer if you are looking for a more challenging hike/run.

Whether you're a Santa Barbara local, new to town or visiting, Inspiration Point is the hike to do! As an SB local myself, Inspiration has always been my favorite. I usually meet a friend at the Mission, then take one car to park on Mission Canyon as spaces can be sparse. Make sure you stay to the left in the beginning to avoid the dead end steep hill! I would say the hike is fairly moderate, with a breathtaking and well deserved view at the top! Bring water!

Clean hike for a breathtaking view of the city. From mountains to ocean there was a great reward at the top. 


Hike is average difficulty and very clean. 

There are plenty of people on the trail and parking on the side up to the entrance. 

It's a quick trip up and back...worth a shot and not to be missed.

So sad. I grew up in this neighborhood before smartphones and it used to  be quiet and rural. Now there are a tons of tourists and students every day. The trails and road are now ruined with loud people, dog feces, crowds, and a horrible parking situation. I wish it could be local again.

Luckily for us, we know someone who lives just below the start of the trail head so we were fortunate to pass by all the other hikers trudging up the hill to get to the head. I'm not sure I'd commit to the hike if I had to anticipate the parking struggle. The extra trudging can add a good extra 15 minutes uphill.

The first 30 minutes of the hike is on pavement with some views of the valley and ocean and one bridge over a creek: not too exciting, but worth it for the greenery and creek beds that come next. 
The trail splits off into different routes (a map is provided at the forks). Our favorite route goes to what is called 3 pools. It's 30 minutes past the first run in with the creek bed. The beginning gets your lungs breathing heavy but after those 10 minutes it's a fun 20 minute dance along the rocky trail DOWN to the pools. (That's right, DOWN, no more uphill. Hooray!) it's a great spot for a picnic if you plan ahead, unlike us.

Our french bulldog easily navigated the Rocky trails and was having a total blast off leash, but be careful at some of the view points because it could be an easy tumble down for your furry friend.
This hike was a lot harder than we anticipated. The birthday girl made a call to turn back before we reached the very top.  We were most certainly inspired to take a nap after we finished. :)

Wow nice area...but lot's of pet waste, really people??

Great views ... free parking.

Great beach that is dog friendly. Park and walk a quarter mile down to the beach. It's not too crowded and the waves aren't big but they do crash near the shore. Perfect place to escape to if ojai is too hot or you just need a nice relaxing day.
beautiful place to relax and enjoy life. This beach is gorgeous and very vacant.

This place is such a slice of heaven, that I don't even know why I am talking about it!

If you are going north on the 101, you would use Exit 88 Padero and go left, then (instead of going left again onto Santa Clause Lane) go over the train tracks and right along a lovely 25mph street until you see the Loon Point parking area on your right.  You park there, go to the north/west end and follow a path under the overpass, behind a home or two and down an access road to the beach.  The beach backs up to some cliffs and you can walk quite a way up and down the coast during low tide, but the beach is quite small/intimate at high tide.

If you are coming South on the 101 you would use Exit 90, go right and then a quick left and you will see the parking lot.  Follow the directions above.

Quiet.  Serene.  Beautiful gliding pelicans.  The sparkle of the sun on the blue ocean currents.  Rocky cliff formations.  Sigh.

Forget I told you about this place, please....

Well I don't spend a lot of time thinking about things like this, it has fascinated me a few times that Horse owners are not required to pick up after their animals, but dog owners are.  I'm sure some scientific analysis of herbivore crap vs. carnivore crap contributed to this, especially considering a horse will pinch off A lot more waste than your average dog.

Here at loon point, One can contemplate this and so many other things having to do with horse and dog poop... Chiefly because there is so much of both all over the place.  Ideally, dog owners would pick up after their pets, but it just seems like that's too much work for most people.  I am considering the number of horses that ride by here, there is plenty of horse manure as well.

Like most spots along the California coast line, you can see the horizon, and the sun dipping below it at sunset.  Multimillion dollar homes line The Cliff along the point, and while pelicans and Seigels are more common, occasionally a few cormorants will happen by.  These marine birds are more or less related to Loons, which is maybe why the spot got its name? Who knows.

Anyway, this place is off the beaten path enough that you can occasionally find a parking spot here, although you're better off just heading up the coast to SB beaches or Isla Vista or Lake Cachuma or something.  Go somewhere else. Riffraff and idiots are generally not tolerated by the local homeowners, who have no problem calling Santa Barbara County Sheriff.  How about homeowners define riffraff and idiots, is often a question of point of view... as in, the point of view from the seaside patio of my coastal mansion.
Have to walk About 3 miles to get down to the beach.  Sand is filled with dog/horse poo and trash.  Lots of homeless live by the train track.  Very isolated.
This is a beautiful quiet little beach with a horse trail access to the beach.

We definitely had a fantastic time here. Hubby and I came here accidentally on our way to Solvang. Last time we were in the area, we discovered a secret beach in Summerland. We got off on an exit too early, and ended up in the parking lot for Loon Point. Free parking and a port-o-potty on site. Although I'm not a fan of those tiny toilets, at least they weren't too dirty.

We had to walk a bit further down the road, under an overpass to get to the beach. A little gross because it looks like people take their horses through here, and leave the poop behind. There was a poop mound bigger than my sister's dog, on the path, and it looked fresh too, made within an hour ago. 

The beach did not disappoint. Cool water, but not unbearable on an early Saturday morning in late summer. Quite a few gross sand fleas where we chose to sit at first, but we quickly moved away within a few minutes. There were very few people there. A few couples and their large dogs, a sunbather sleeping in the corner, and a couple who were getting married along with their officiator and photographer capturing the scene. I turned to my husband and dreamily told him how great it would've been if we had a private intimate ceremony on the beach together instead of our wedding. It would've been really sweet. 

We spent about an hour there, getting in the water, watching dogs run into the ocean to get the balls their owners throw, and sunbathing. Then we packed up and headed back to the car. Overall, a nice experience. Very quiet private beach with a nice view of the shoreline you see when you pass by PCH. Would've given this place 5 stars had it not been for the sand fleas and the horse waste. At least we know better now.  

I recently visited the secret beach we found in Summerland and it's absolutely surrounded by rocks everywhere making it nearly unwalkable. Maybe this will be mine and hubby's new secret beach. Hope to return soon.

The most important consideration of Santa Barbara beaches is tar. Where is it this year and how bad. Butterfly beach is bad this summer. Globs. Loon Point is one the cleanest. There is no animal "waste " on the beach but the trail down can be after the horses pass through which they do at 5 pm.                                                           
              Northern part of the beach still retains its european flavor on weekdays or during the off-season.

This place was a gem! Quiet and easy to access. Free parking as well. Went on a Monday morning and barely came across anyone. 

When you enter the parking lot, the train is at the very end of the lot. You'll see a sign that says to access. Then it looks like you're walking to the train tracks, but only about 50ft. You'll need to turn left to go up the small hill. 

Watch out for some horse or dog poop on the ground!
Our family loves this beach, but our cars were broken into by parking in the parking lot, by thefts drilling out the drivers side locks..BEWARE, do not leave anything of value in your car. 10/3/2020

This beach view is a tucked away gem that takes a bit of a hike to reach. We popped in about 30 minutes before the parking lot access closed and the beach was empty. It appears horses are allowed here as there was a lot of horse manure on the path to get to the beach. The actual beach area was pretty short- maybe this depends on the tide, but it was a beautiful tranquil peaceful little spot with plenty of parking in the nearby lot.

The San Marcos Foothills Preserve is a County of Santa Barbara open space located in the foothills between Santa Barbara and Goleta. Encompassing 200 acres (plus a 10 acres park) the Preserve offers extraordinary views of the Santa Ynez Mountains, Pacific Ocean, and the Channel Islands. 

A web guide to the Preserve can be found here:  www.sanmarcosfoothills.com
The Preserve is the feeding ground for hawks, kestrels, kites, roadrunners, coyotes, bobcats, and hundreds of other animal species. It offers nesting locations for dozens of native bird species, and it provides connecting corridors for wildlife to Los Padres National Forest and Goleta Slough.

There is a well maintained, 2.6 mile trail that runs between the Preserve western entrance at Via Gaitero Road and the Preserve Eastern entrance at Antone Road. A side trail runs a short distance to the north in the middle of the Preserve and leads to a lookout that provides panoramic views of Santa Barbara and Goleta plus the Santa Ynez Mountains, Pacific Ocean, and the Channel Islands. Also, many species of wildflowers are visible during the late winter and spring season. Follow the links at left to view a trail map and for a list of plants that grow on the Preserve.

This is a wildlife PRESERVE not a park, and as a result, there are some restrictions on the kinds of recreation that are allowed there.  The Preserve is open for hiking and walking etc., but bikes and horses are not allowed.  Please see this web page for more info: http://www.sanmarcosfoothills.com/bikes.html.  Please keep your dog on a leash for the safety of your pet and the wildlife.

Beautiful spot, but ruined by overzealous park ranger that drives 2 miles in to cite me for having my old dog unleashed (i was carrying the leash). I understand it is the rule there, but there was no other person on the trail that day and i was the only car parked at the trail head. How about a warning? or citing the person on the side of the rode as i was leaving the trail that threw his cigarette onto the ground by the brush.

This is my new outdoor hike that's close to home! Hidden just off of Highway 154 is the San Marcos Foothills Preserve. It's a beautiful oasis with ocean, island and mountain view's featuring hiking/running trails. While the main trail is a 3.7-mile loop that's of moderate difficulty with a gain of 751 feet, multiple smaller unmarked trails venture further into the reaches of the preserve. There's a large parking lot right at the trailhead (for exact directions use: 34.457620, -119.766830). The best feature about this preserve is the fact that one section is maintained by a herd of 400 sheep with two shepherds and several sheepdogs from the Channel Islands Foundation. I was lucky enough to run into them during my adventure. Truly happy California sheep--forget about cows! The trail is not for horses or bicycles, however, is dog friendly if they remain leashed. I saw some great wildlife besides the sheep including a skink, lizards, a gopher and many birds species. Check it out for yourself.

This is one of my favorite spots. It's quiet, peaceful, and safe. I come here whenever I'm in the area to do reflecting or enjoy the scenery. There is a clear pathway, but you can definitely go and hide out in the field if you wanted.
Really nice hike that doesn't require driving up a long and windy road. Tons of parking available and pretty! Not too much shade but it's an ok hike! Not too difficult either

This is a nice little hike if you only have 45 minutes or so. The trail meanders over gentle hills of open grassland. There is no tree cover so it would be hot in summer but this breezy day in April it was quite pleasant. Dogs must be leashed.

This was a great spot for a hike in town. There are some hills but I'd say it is moderate level for a hike, nothing too difficult. We went in November so it wasn't super green, but the ocean views throughout the hike are incredible. You get a sweeping view of the coast of Goleta as well as the Santa Barbara coast. 

When we did this hike we didn't see too many other people out walking, but everyone we did see was wearing a mask so we felt very safe on this walk/hike. We'll definitely be coming again for an afternoon hike with beautiful views!!

Easy area to hike in and welcoming to dogs. There is parking right at the beginning which is easy access. The view of the ocean behind you or the hills above you is perfect for a morning stroll or sunset hike. There isn't that much shade so recommend a hat or going in not so hot hours. Nice thing is you feel pretty safe here and there are people around so you can go alone if you like; just keep an eye on your surroundings as with any outdoor space. Great location too since it is close to town!

The walk here is not at all difficult.  Fairly flat with a couple of dips.  What is unique about this preserve are the poisonous plants in abundance.  When I was a kid, my parents had castor bean trees and oleander bushes around the house.  They said, "Don't eat them.  They are poisonous."  I smiled to myself thinking back that I did everything they asked me not to do except eat the poisonous plants.

I first came across the Datura wrightii https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datura_wrightii and took lots of photos - only to discover they are everywhere.  These plants are unusual because they grow lush leaves and large beautiful trumpet flowers in places that are so dry and on hard packed ground that it is surprising they can do so well.

The castor bean tree https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ricinus is what is used to make castor oil - or as my brothers enjoyed doing - having castor bean fights with the green castor beans that are perfect for throwing at each other coming in nice bunches to carry around as an ammunition cluster.

This area is under some sort of study.  Areas have been marked and are under observation.  You can see the stakes and the tags on the stakes that identify the plots.  I use to do the plot studies years ago for the BLM.  The plots are photographed each year and the plants are identified.  This way, one might discover a trend over time.  My study site was south-eastern Oregon.  What did we learn?  Nothing really changes out there.

San Marcos Foothill preserve is a 4 hour investment of your time.  If you enjoy running, this is probably a good place to get a run in because it's open and the trails are in good condition.

name: Mindbody Surf and Yoga School
categories: Meditation Centers, Education, Summer Camps, Surfing, Yoga, Fitness & Instruction, Surf Shop, Active Life, Surf Schools, Shopping, Specialty Schools, Fashion, Swimming Lessons/Schools, Paddleboarding

yelp_reviews.text: Lindsay has years of experience of surfing , she definitely  is a professional.  My first lesson with her at the age 45 was fun and  easy I give her a ten!  very good instructor.

This was the best experience I have had with a surf instructor! She was so patient and gave really good insight on how to safely catch a wave and stand up. I would 100% recommend this surf school for any age group and experience level. It's a guaranteed great time !

This was my first surf lesson and I really enjoyed my time out in the water. I gained confidence paddling out and learning proper techniques , the instructor is very knowledgeable and kept the atmosphere positive. Overall this surf school is perfect for all ages and I would highly recommend giving this school a try!
Great location for learning to surf. I would recommend this school to beginners, the coaches do no give up until you get to your feet!

Took a trip to Santa Barbara and wanted to try this surfing thing out. Booked a lesson with Lindsay, she was so nice and encouraging. I had the best time. I felt safe the entire time. Lindsay knew so much about water safety and how to get in the right spot to catch a wave. I had so much fun and will definitely be booking another lesson the next time I'm in Santa Barbara. Highly recommend booking a lesson with Mindbody surf school wether you are new to the water or just need a little refresher. Plus Santa Barbara is beautiful so how could you miss.

HORRIBLE!!!!! Worst service ever!!!!!
Ditched on the street before the lesson ever started! 
Totally not recommended! The bitch named Lindsey ask for a$50 credit card payment prior to the lesson. The guy she told me will give me the lesson was filthy with a broken filthy board, and when I asked about the suit the Lindsey supposedly told him to get (after she took my size over the phone) was not a women's suit and not my size! He asked me to get into the water without it but I refused and when hopelessly I tried to squeeze myself in a suit that is 3 sizes smaller, it got stuck half way through! I was furious! And I told him that this is not the service I expected for $100. I told him I want to cancel the class, then he got so mad at me and he literally ditch me in the street in a location I'm not familiar with since I'm just a tourist. 
Tried to call Lindsey a thousand times and she stopped replying. I ended up canceling my credit card and taking the action through my back.
DO NOT TRUST these people , 
The worst service on planet earth!!!! 
Soooooooooooooo disappointed! 
I bet they either paid those who wrote good reviews or just made up accounts to write reviews themselves like this very last one "Julie D" posted how good Lindsey is hours ago :( so pathetic....,

Last summer we took a family vacation to California. While in Santa Barbara we decided to live like the locals and take a surf lesson. We took turns because we have little ones, but we had a blast! I was really nervous and didn't think I could do it, but Lindsay was so positive and encouraging. I stood up and caught a small wave on my first lesson! My husband did even better, he stood up his second try. My older son who was 2 at the time had so much fun laying on the surf board and trying to stand up.  I would highly recommend Lindsay as your surf instructor for a surfing experience to never forget! We just booked our trip for this summer! Can't wait for another surf lesson!

name: Rincon Beach Park
categories: Active Life, Beaches, Swimming Pools, Parks

yelp_reviews.text: Beautiful!!! Sunsets, surfers, tide pools, and good beachcombing for shells!

This by far is our favorite beach to go to with our son! It's about a 50 mile drive from where we live but oh so worth it. 

First of all the parking area is free and has a spectacular view! You have to climb down a few stairs to get to the isolated beach. It's quite a stretch and it's perfect for little kids to play and run around. My son enjoys the little waves, the sandy beaches. 

So next time you plan a beach day try heading out to this spot or not. You will not be disappointed.

Rincon had waves and we enjoyed them for what they where at 2 to 3' iregular shape . We paddled alot which means we  surfed alot with long  100 yard rides.  Long rides= long paddling back out. After over a dozen rides it's time for my coffee so we proceed to get some.
Winter has been so bad that Rincon did not go off when it's typical to do so. So I can say that Rincon made my very happy today . 
Aloha

Every time I drive down south on the 101, I never fail to exit Seacliffe, park my car, and watch the waves crash against the beach (and depending on the tide and weather, against the rocks).  Beautiful serene setting and perfect place to watch the sunset.

Absolutely beautiful! My family beach camps along Seacliffe once or twice a year. It is breathtaking. The beach is always clean, water is clean, and we always see dolphins playing out in the water. The only downfall is that everyone else loves it too so depending on when you go, it is really hard to get a spot to camp. You also have to climb up and down rocks to get to the beach so it would not be ideal for people unable to do so. No tent camping allowed.

Great surf beach! The rocks on the winter shore are beautiful! Lots of little tide pools and some wildlife! 

This beach is picturesque partly nestled in the cliffs and partly bordered by lovely beach houses.

Gorgeous beach, gorgeous surf, gorgeous surfers, and lovely sunsets!
I love that this beach is super close to my house, about a 5 minute drive if that.
Great place to go watch a sunset, go dolphin sighting, or just lay out and relax!!!

We accidently came upon this spot to avoid the LA traffic back to our home in Oceanside and it was such a private, relaxing family and pet friendly beach! After the easy trek down the side of the cliff, we plopped ourselves next to the shore and felt a nice cool breeze.  Lots to look at with passing horseback riders, happy dogs catching balls and parasailers cruising and landing above us. The bathroom and shower facilities were decent too.
Great place for fishing  and has a pretty decent point break for surfing  if you're into that sort of thing. Love  watching the sunrise  as well as sunset.

Nice non touristy beach. Great place to sun, swim and play around with the family without all of the crowds. It's our beach of choice! We almost never go to east beach or the like anymore. Not that I'm a beachy person anyways!

I'm not sure if this is a "dog beach" per se, but we took our 3 dogs here on a very hot Monday( yesterday).  The parking is free and the walk down the "boat ramp" is pretty easy.  We had a beach cart that was loaded to the gills and it was easy to bring down.  There is a lot of seaweed on the beach.  There were quite a few flies/bugs that were annoying( possibly due to the seaweed).  I wish they would sweep the beach, maybe they do in the summer?   My dogs had a great time.
Very chill, beautiful flowers, not crowded, great waves, and lots of good surfers, paddle boarders and grizzled veterans hanging out.  Perfect getaway beach.

Mid January, 5:30 pm - sunset.

Park in the lot, careful because google maps won't get you there properly.  There's a surfers parking lot and a hidden beach.  To the right is a sign to the park and the left are trails of wet footprints.  I went one of the better directions. 

Through what looks like a makeshift jungle trail to the shore.  There it is- Hawaii.  So it could pass of it was way warmer.  Awesome views, cute little locals surfer hut with a firepit and plenty of surfers out on the beach and the water.  You won't find any tourists there and if you are one, try to blend in...but...you won't because you won't have a wetsuit.  Choose a log, sit and watch.

Great great spot.  Having gone to many braces all over CA and HI, I have high beach expectations- this one by far one of the best.  Next time, I'm bringing my glasses so I can see the black spots on all the waves better.
Nice little spot to hang. Quiet and very pretty.  Good beach access through the stairs. Nice spot to bbq as well. Will definitely be back to this place again.
This is a great little beach. I loved the minimal feel to it, just the ocean and you.
The 'top 10' in the world? That's not really credible since all I got was feet full of tar today. As beautiful as it is it is such a shame to be so polluted in the sand. Damn those oil rigs!

Hey All, 
We have been camping hear at Faria Beach/Rincon for 15 years.
This year there has been an unusual amount of these bugs all over the rocks.
Question 1,
What are they?
Question 2,
Do they bite?

Another beautiful beach in Carpinteria! The shoreline is amazing.
The beach is dog friendly and there is parking available.  I was there during the week so there were plenty of spots open.

There is a place to BBQ, but I'm not sure if you'd need to reserve it or not!

I wouldn't recommend Rincon to someone that might have difficulty with stairs as you'll have to use them to access the beach!

A couple people compared this beach to Hawaii.  Still laughing.  The worst beaches in Hawaii are still about 10 times better than rincon, but I guess when you stay on the mainland too long every beach starts feeling like Hawaii?  There are a few decent areas at Rincon, around the point, but most of Rincon kinda blows chunks(especially closer to the RV parkway), and I don't mean chunks of oil, which I heard do vomit up here on occasion.  Some reviewers on another site also mentioned the charm/nostalgia of the train rolling by, which I guess is another thing that becomes charming when you lose your marbles ;-)

No review of California surf beaches would be complete without a tip of the hat and a bow to the queen herself, Rincon.

Rincon, the "little corner" by the sea, possesses one of the finest waves anywhere, bar none. This is California's tame version of South Africa's famous Jeffrey's Bay and it's why it has earned the honorific of the "Queen of the Coast". And rightfully so. Catch a big, outside wave here and you'll never forget it. With the right swell, you can take a wave from the outer reef and ride it all the way to the freeway, one of the longest rides you'll ever get and your legs will feel like  rubber when you finally kick-out. A quarter mile? It'll feel like it!

The shape of the wave is ideal for both short or long-boarders and both species will be vying for the perfect take-off position. While never as crowded or aggressive as Malibu, it does get packed at times and you should be prepared to stand up for your right to surf.  Show respect and, most of the time, it will be returned. 

With internet weather projections and beach cams available at the tip of your finger, everyone knows about the approaching swell and it's getting more crowded out there every time...something you just have to get used to. 

So while Rincon will always offer one of the best waves in the US, it's no cake-walk and you'll have to earn your place in the line-up. If you're shy, you might as well stay home. However, there's always some good news: All the homes in the area  have old. leaky septic tank sewage systems. That means they leach waste sewage right into the surf zone. Can you spell "giardia"? Many people won't surf here after a storm...some people don't want to get sick. I know, I was side-lined for a week after surfing a big ground-swell a couple years ago, on the tail end of a storm. So pollution can be your friend, in a sick, twisted way.

"Hey Robin, was it worth it?" you ask. 

"Oh, hell yeah!" (big grin)

(it'll be a perfect score when they replace the septic systems)

My mother turned me onto Rincon, telling me it is mentioned in the Beach Boy's Surfing Safari for a reason. "At Rincon they're walking the nose." My first drive out of Santa Barbara once I had my license was here to my parent's fear.

I take the small side trail to the beach that others have mentioned and relax watching some great surfers at High Tide.

Its a very relaxing trip but out of town surfers beware. Locals here can get territorial.

I've been to a lot of beaches, it's hard to give a beach anything but 5 stars because ... it's a beach!  It's better than the 100+ degree weather at home.   My family and I stopped at this particular beach based on the good yelp reviews.   It was an otherwise fine beach except 1 particular detail.  It's got oil all over the place.   It's not 100% black or anything, but walking along the beach you see what look like little dime to half dollar sized rocks that are just a bit shiny, they're drops of oil.   Looking online I see there was an oil spill a bit over a year ago, but some people reported that was cleaned up 6+ months ago.  You see what look to be offshore drilling rigs out in the distance, while I was there I just assumed it was from those. Anyway, the oil will get all over your feet.   If you go out in the surf, tiny little freckle looking spots of oil will get all over your skin/bathing suit.   Getting this stuff off is a nightmare, it sticks to you like glue.   Luckily, one of the experienced beachgoers saw me trying to rub it off with sand and gave me a tip that sunscreen and a bit of sand works better and she was right, we had some aerosol sunscreen that seemed to help cut the oil.   That said, we got back home and everyone had to scrub up to get the little freckle size dots we missed at the beach and some of the bathing suits we have are stained now.   This is the first time I've experienced oil/tar on a beach and it's also the first time I've left a beach and agreed with my family that we probably wouldn't ever go back, which is a shame because it was an otherwise nice beach.

Took my surf baby, surfer husband and 4 month old here. Loved the beach. It appeals to the young, the old, serious long boarders, feather weight long boarders and dog owners. People were BBQing in a covered little patio area, it also offered tables with umbrellas... the beach was somewhat crowded but plenty of space to choose your spot and camp. Lots of kids for my older one to play with and some shady spots to hang with the baby. The inside had some small waves that my little one could ride (with dad) and outside had bigger waves for dad. Only problem for us was the walk down, unless you have a stroller that can go off roading down a bumpy trail learn from us and drop your stuff off at the mouth of the trail to carry down as parking isn't abundant nearby so if youre rolling deep with little ones take them from the top of trail to avoid dead arm.

I absolutely love this beach.. My husband and I come here about 5-6 times a year and cant wait to come back.  We have a trailer we park right along side the beach and have a front row view of the dolphin show every morning. We usually stay for 4-5 days at a time. It is very private.. It almost seems like u have your own private beach!

It is first come first serve.. So on a holiday, you MAY NOT find a spot to park your RV or Trailer.. be prepared to turn around and camp somewhere locally  and try again the next morning (if your determined to stay here). 

I love this place for its calmness. This is NOT the place for noisey party go-ers. There is usually an older crowd or families that frequent this particular beachside. 

Although, i have heard there is some theft that goes on after dark at times so i suggest always locking up anything of value.. bikes, generators, etc..  luckily, we havn't had any incidents. 

Even the winter days and nights are gorgeous!! But im a beach lover :)

I have so much more to add but i gotta go!       I'll be back soon!!
This is an awesome place to bike!!!‍‍‍love it!!!!with ocean breeze fresh air..Great place!

This beach is gorgeous! Located just in between the hustle and bustle of Ventura's and Carpenteria's main beaches, it's the perfect beach for a day of just hanging out with your pals, furry or otherwise.

The beach is located down a path below the parking lot, showers, bathrooms, and shaded tables, which are always reserved for some type of event whenever I've been. It's probably because the view from up there is just so beautiful.

Down at the beach you will encounter lots of driftwood, seashells, washed up seaweed and sand fleas all over it, and of course DOGGOS. There are doggos and doggos aplenty at this beach, probably because it's one of the few around that allows them. I take my dog here for some nice walks around the waters' edge (he dislikes the water but loves the beach, weirdo). 

So come to chill, maybe pick up some driftwood for some crafts you've got in mind, and be sure to pet all of the puppers that pass by because dogs are the reason for living in this strange world.

O! And there's always cool little sculptures of rocks and driftwood laying around so add to the free art!

Love this place beach front property! Smooth waves and happy times! Trains come by every 3o min just across the street! Nice place hard to get a spot tho! They have 130 rv spots. More are coming soon tho! Can't say enough just what a awesome place.
This place is amazing. And never disappoints!!! The sunset's are always amazing. Rain or shine,  usually your neighbor's are friendly and it is a great place to just come and chill
I love this place. We went with friends and stayed 5 nights. Only thing now is you have to book in advance.

Stopped over here to take a business phone call.  Great place to just stop and "take a moment".  For the surfer community, this is a great access point down to Rincon Beach via paved path way.  Nice little place to take a break, enjoy a coffee (bring your own) or enjoy your (own) lunch.  Stop in and enjoy the ocean.
This place is amazing. Free parking, lots of room to spread out, amazing driftwood and a wide gentle beach. Great place to bring your dog too!

This is a great beach for boogie boarding and surfing... Last 3 visits waves have been huge, the water warm and clear. There is a bathroom in the park which is nice, only one, but that's more than some of the other popular beaches in the area, also there is a fair amount of parking. The only downside to this beach is the long trip down the hill via stairs or ramp,making it a challenge to carry all out stuff down, and keeping me from bringing my paddle board.

This is, by far, the hottest and most popular surf spot in the area, as much for the beauty as for the reality that from Goleta to Carpenteria the surf is flat because of those pesky Channel Islands that block the waves out so this is about the breaking point where the waves start to roll in. You don't know who you might run into on any given morning out on this spot. It can get a little bit crowded though and if you are a novelty long board surfer like myself, it can be a little bit intimidating, if not just hard to catch a wave when you look over and realize that you are staring down Kelly Slater or some other pro!! ha ha  I spend a lot of my time down at La Conchita personally because it's quiter and easier to catch endless waves, but that's just me. I never turn Rincon down though either!

Picturesque beach with great tide pools and a gorgeous sunset. Definitely a surf spot. Friendly dogs out and about. A lovely beach to visit with conscientious beachgoers who pick up their trash, leaving a tide pool biodiversity that is fun to explore.

One of the best beach we've gone so far since leaving in LA. It's about 1.5hrs drive from Pasadena on a weekday. 
Beach is nice, clean, and not crowded on weekdays. If you decide to go weekends, it's decent, just walk to the left, further out and you are still social distancing. People respect your space. We came here twice already, both times with our pup. She loves it. Walking between the rocks with shallow waters. You might have a bit of tar on your feet or but you can take it off with baby oil. You will not be disappointed.

An easy hike with lots of great views.  Rarely crowded.  You MUST stay for the sunsetting over Goleta and the airport.  Afterwards head down the hill to Cold Spring Tavern for dinner.
Don't forget to have an Adventure Pass or you risk a ticket because it's in the National Forest.

It's really alot of fun, but is a busy place. 

Not really a hike per se, more of how much work out you really want to do.

Views are just crazy good.

The end to a perfect trip. Climbiing around the rocks on Lizards mouth trail. Sometimes something so simple can be so much fun. 
I came to SB because I missed my friend. I was thankful she took me here to show me what natural beauty the area had to offer. We hiked until sunset. Climbing up and through rocks, weaving through ridges, and jumping from peak to peak. I will never forget the moments I had here. Re connecting with one of my best friends. We stopped on a high rock to sit down and stare in awe at the beaches and land below. We reminisced about old times and laughed about boys. In an insane time of my life this was the sanity I needed. It was beauty, clarity and friendship all at the same time. I realized high on top of these massive rocks that I loved my life. I loved my self. And most of all, I realized its ok to be who you are.

-good times with hard core motherfuckerbiatching climbers
-kinda hard to find if you've never been
-you know you're on top of the lizard's head when you see the lion king looking hill (where the monkey dood lifts the cub)
-the lizard's mouth was heaven for the climbers
-it was ideal since it was a warm day and it was fully shaded!
=I PEED HERE TWICE

A short hike, but a long and twisty drive. Be ready. Positioned close to a gun range, so you hear shots fired now and then, is a large collection of boulders. Aka, Lizards Mouth. That are really fun to climb on and see the city from. A little dangerous, if your not a mountain climbing person. But if you are into the adventure, perfect. Fun way to get out of the city.
One of my favorite hiking spots in S.B. The views are fantastic and it's not crowded on weekdays.
Incredible place. So much to see you can't do it in one visit. It's not exactly a hike, more like a big playground dotted with beautiful sandstone boulders.

Beautiful park with amazing views all around. I have been out here a few times for bouldering and hiking and have really enjoyed myself each time. 

I will say there have been times where I have come across broken beer bottles and cigarettes but they are usually cleaned up by the next time I am out there. 

The gun club up the road is a bit discouraging to hear, but no fatalities yet! ;)

Super fun hike! For a while we couldn't find the actual lizards mouth, but venturing around is just as fun!!!! Keep in mind that the mouth is deep into the trail. We went straight forward and around to the left, while the mouth is far right. Enjoy :)
There's no capturing this place with a wordy description... just get out and enjoy it!

Spectacular view, fun walk, lots of rock hopping. Sadly also lots of graffiti and trash. I was stupid to think something so far away would keep out the asshats that leave nature marked up and trashed. The trails hiker maintained and trails seem to lead all over the place. I fun fallowing random ones until the dead ended. Lots of neat little corners and picnic spots.

The hike was hard to find in the beginning. But once we found it. It was an easy hike. It is about a 0.5 mile hike to the lizards mouth. Alot of rock climbing and saw alot of lizards running around. Went on a day that was 102+, but still made it without passing out. So that tells you it was an easy hike. Up steep or up hill climb. 

This hike was right before the gun club fyi.

This is an amazing place to explore and discover. It isn't much of a trail, more like big rocks to climb and see the view of Santa Barbara and the Pacific Ocean. If you are in this area this is definitely worth checking out. Did it with my husband and almost three year old. We all loved it!

our party included:  a 90 lb dog, 5 year old, 3 year old, and 3 adults on a October weekend -- so it wasnt ridiculously hot.  Dont forget to bring munchies for the kiddo's and hella water for everyone.  

trailhead is well marked on the left hand side of road.  if you hit gravel -- you went too far.  if you start from the actual trailhead, the trail is decently marked / trodden.  if you start before the actual trailhead, you will be finding your own trail along the boulders.  

we didnt encounter any broken glass,  thankfully.  

Our whole party made it to the top / lizzard's mouth.  cool view.

If you're into graffiti covered rocks, trash everywhere, and dirtbag ravers, then this is the place for you. I swear, Los Padres Forest Service needs to install a locked gate down the road in an attempt to keep the human garbage out. This is why I go backpacking deep into the wilderness; to get away from terrible people. End rant.
very good hike, especially if you go down to paradise falls and up to lizard rock, beautiful views and yes there are lizards lol

This place was kinda hard to find. We followed directions here and trip advisor but we didn't end up at the entrance of the lizards mouth sign. It's kinda hidden. So we just started up a trail before. We also got lost and had to figure out own way to the lizards mouth. It's actually a really short hike if you start at the right point, but we didn't. Once we got up there it was pretty cool. You have to go to the side and around and down some rocks to try to get a good picture of the mouth. But once you're there it was cool. The walk back was much easier.

This is an incredible place to rock hop, boulder, and explore.

The one negative is that it is right next to a gun club, which (for me) takes away a star. I don't enjoy hearing gunfire while being out in "nature." The views are certainly worth it though.

Drive wasn't that bad, hike itself was easy! In 99 degree weather it was a bit challenging but ultimately worth it! The view from the top is gorgeous and the pictures from the top of the lizard mouth is insane. A must do in SB!

There are so many alternate vantage points that you can easily avoid the crowds of teenagers at sunset. The view is fantastic, in case the winding drive up didn't clue you in. It took us almost an hour to figure out the proper way to ascend the infinite scramble options, but only 20 minutes back to the road. A lot of fun if you don't mind unmarked paths, a bit of challenge, and the occasional rabbit and some stubborn lizards. It gets dark fast, so I recommend bringing a light source for your return trip, and a light jacket if it's a windy day. The graffiti is certainly unfortunate at most vantage points, and watch out for broken glass between the boulders.

My experience at the Lizzards Mouth trail was easy and fun! It was a quick hike into the trail and if you keep going you will see an amazing view! I seen amazing views from every angle! One of the first must hike trail! I spent 3 hours at Lizzards Mouth! Loved the view of the sunset from here.
Super easy hike with great views. If you're into rock climbing this is the perfect place for you. However, watching out for lizards and snakes in this area.

The one word that gets me here every time is SUNSET! The best time to come and the most beautiful attraction this spot can provide is the amazing Santa Barbara sunset. I've also heard sunRISE is pretty spectacular but generally I'm more interested in the views of my inner eyelids at that time.

Just to get one thing straight real quick, this is not a hike. Do not come here if you are looking for a strenuous trek or some form of walking/jogging/uphill workout. No, this is a drive up into the mountains on the 154, turning right on Kinevan Rd/W Camino Cielo, driving another 3.67 miles along a windy West Camino Cielo Rd, parking and walking to the rocks where you can climb around and play hide and seek.
